title: Revelation 20
intro:
Rev 20 is the **millennium chapter** — the core SDA eschatological hinge between the Second Coming (Rev 19:11-21) and the eternal new-earth state (Rev 21-22). Pioneer SDA reads the thousand years as a **heaven-located** period BETWEEN the Second Coming (when the righteous dead are raised in the **first resurrection**, the living righteous are translated, and the wicked are slain by the brightness of Christ's coming) and the **third coming** (when the New Jerusalem descends to earth and the executive judgment / second death is poured out on the resurrected wicked). During the 1000 years the earth lies **desolate** — a vast charnel-house — with no living human inhabitants; Satan is "bound" not with a literal chain but by a **chain of circumstances** (the righteous are out of his reach in heaven, the wicked are in their graves, and he has no one left to deceive); the "bottomless pit" is the abyssal pre-creation chaos to which the earth has been reduced (per Gen 1:2 LXX _abussos_ = Rev 20:1 _abussos_); the saints reign with Christ in the heavenly New Jerusalem and execute the millennial investigative-judgment-of-the-wicked (1 Cor 6:1-3) determining the degree of punishment for each lost person. At the end of the millennium: the **second resurrection** of the wicked (v. 5b, v. 13); Satan "loosed" by getting his power-base back; the New Jerusalem descends to earth (GC 662-666 = Rev 21:2 read chronologically before Rev 21:1); the resurrected wicked, under Satan, surround the city (Gog and Magog of vv. 8-9); **fire from God** devours them in the lake-of-fire executive judgment; death and hell themselves are cast in — the **second death** = annihilation, not eternal torment.

Bohr aligns with this pioneer SDA framework on **every primary identification** — abyss = pre-creation chaos of the desolate earth; binding of Satan = no one left to deceive (the captivity-by-loss-of-power-base argument paralleled to Rev 13:9-10); first resurrection = righteous-only at the second coming; saints reign and judge in heaven 1000 years (1 Cor 6:1-3); Satan loosed = second resurrection of his power-base; Gog and Magog = resurrected wicked of all ages; New Jerusalem descends to earth before the wicked surround it; fire from God = executive judgment / second death = annihilation; lake of fire = same event as vv. 8-9 (vv. 14-15 reaches the same climax as vv. 8-9). Bohr's distinctive structural move is the **four-cycle recapitulation framework** for Rev 20:1-21:8 (Cycle 1 = 20:1-3 Satan + earth; Cycle 2 = 20:4-10 the saints; Cycle 3 = 20:11-21:1 judgment of the wicked; Cycle 4 = 21:2-8 the New Jerusalem) — a Rule-VI recapitulation reading, fully consonant with Smith's chronological framework. Source: Bohr, _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 261-279 (Lessons on Rev 20-21:1), with cross-coverage at pp. 537, 541, 3367-3370, 5911, 6017, 6029-6138, 6568, 8756-8838, 10133-10144, 10531, 10939, 11007-11260. This is **one of Bohr's most rule-compliant chapters** — pioneer + Bohr in near-lockstep on the core SDA millennium doctrine.

verses.text: And I saw an angel come down from heaven, having the key of the bottomless pit and a great chain in his hand.

pioneerReadings.html:
EGW reads the descending angel through the Lev 16 scapegoat lens, identifying the binding scene as the antitypical sending-away of the great High Priest’s adversary. “When the work of atonement in the heavenly sanctuary has been completed, then in the presence of God and heavenly angels and the hosts of the redeemed the sins of God’s people will be placed upon Satan; he will be declared guilty of all the evil which he has caused them to commit. And as the scapegoat was sent away into a land not inhabited, so Satan will be banished to the desolate earth, an uninhabited and dreary wilderness” (GC 658.1).

The descending angel arrives after the close of probation, the seven plagues, and the Second Coming have already emptied the earth of living humans (GC 657.1). The “bottomless pit” she identifies with the post-plague earth: “The earth looked like a desolate wilderness. Cities and villages, shaken down by the earthquake, lay in heaps… Here is to be the home of Satan with his evil angels for a thousand years” (EW 290.1). The chain and key are emblems of the authority by which God confines Satan to a ruined planet he can no longer leave.

The angel is **not Christ** but a separate executor of the post-millennial scapegoat-typology act. Smith argues from Lev-16 typology: "**Christ is the great High Priest of this dispensation. On the day of atonement, anciently, two goats were taken by the priest, upon which lots were cast, one for the Lord, and the other for the scape-goat… so by arguments… Satan is shown to be the antitypical scape-goat**" (DAR 687.3). And: "**This we believe to be the very event described in the verses under notice. The sanctuary service is, at the time here specified, closed. Christ lays upon the head of the Devil the sins which have been transferred to the sanctuary… and the Devil is sent away, not by the hand of the High Priest, but by the hand of another person, according to the type, into a place here called the bottomless pit. Hence this angel is not Christ**" (DAR 690.1). On the key + chain: "**It cannot be supposed that the key and chain are literal; they are rather used merely as symbols of the power and authority with which this angel is clothed upon this occasion**" (DAR 690.2).

Miller’s mature post-1843 millennium framework places the binding of Satan and the descending angel of v.1 between two literal resurrections, repudiating both the “temporal” spiritual-millennium scheme and any pre-resurrection golden age. “The ancients believed in a temporal and personal reign of Christ on earth. The moderns believe in a temporal, spiritual reign as a millennium. Both views are wrong—both are too gross and carnal. I believe in a glorious, immortal and personal reign of Jesus Christ with all his people on the purified earth forever. I believe the millennium is between the two resurrections and two judgments: the righteous and the wicked, the just and the unjust” (MWV1 35.2).

Miller does not exegete v.1’s angel or chain in detail, but his rejection of any literal “chain” or pit-of-fire imprisonment is implicit in his framework: the abyss is the desolation-state between the two resurrections, into which Satan is “cast” by the events of Christ’s second appearing. Divergence flag: Miller’s pre-1844 lectures sometimes use “Personal Reign of One Thousand Years” phrasing that James White later cites approvingly (SLWM 90.2) but that Smith / EGW refine by locating the saints in heaven, not on earth, during those 1000 years.

Litch lists the binding of Satan in the “bottomless pit” among the deliverances the saints enjoy at the Second Coming, alongside their incorruption and their reign with Christ. “They shall be delivered from the temptations of the devil, who shall be chained and cast into the bottomless pit, and be shut up until the final resurrection; and even then can have no power on those who have part in the first resurrection” (PREX2 117.2).

Litch’s framing—the “chain” tied to the post-2nd-Advent deliverance of the redeemed—matches Smith’s reading. The descending angel of v.1 is not Christ but an executor whose key-and-chain are emblems of the authority by which Satan is cut off from his work of deception “until the final resurrection.” Litch’s structural list at PREX2 117.2 lays out the same five-point post-Advent deliverance grid (bondage, fall’s evils, Satan’s temptations, pain/death, ungodly retribution) that Smith later organizes around Rev 20.

James White anchors the descending-angel binding scene in the single biblical millennium—the Rev 20 thousand years between the two resurrections. “I found that the only millennium taught in the word of God is the thousand years which are to intervene between the first resurrection and that of the rest of the dead, as inculcated in the twentieth of Revelation; and that it must necessarily follow the personal coming of Christ and the regeneration of the earth” (SLWM 53.1, quoting Miller’s mature view, which J. White endorses as the Adventist standard).

White does not exegete the angel, the key, or the chain in detail in his published works on the second-advent movement; his treatment is framework-level. But by making Rev 20:1-3 the structural opener of the millennium (placed after the personal advent, before the new-earth state), he sets the stage on which Smith, EGW, Haskell, and Bohr all locate the descending-angel act: the literal arrival of an executor to lock Satan into the desolated earth he ruined.

Crosier links the binding act of Rev 20:1-2 directly to the completion of the Day-of-Atonement scapegoat ritual, making the descending angel’s act the antitypical “fit man” transfer of Lev 16:21-22. “This was the legal process, and when fulfilled the author of sins will have received them back again, (but the ungodly will bear their own sins), and his head will have been bruised by the seed of the woman; the ‘strong man armed’ will have been bound by a stronger than he, ‘and his house (the grave) spoiled of its goods (the saints).’ Matthew 12:29… The thousand years imprisonment of Satan will have begun, and the saints will have entered upon their millennial reign with Christ” (SANC 24.2).

Crosier’s 1846 Sanctuary essay is the foundational Sabbatarian-Adventist statement of the heavenly-sanctuary / scapegoat doctrine that Smith, EGW, and Bohr all later restate. Crosier’s contribution at v.1 is to anchor the “chain” in the Matt 12:29 binding-of-the-strong-man saying: the same Christ who at his first advent “bound” the strong man and spoiled his house (rescued the dead saints) now, through his agent, binds Satan finally on a desolated earth.

Haskell stages the descending angel against the wider sweep of Satan’s three falls—cast out of heaven, cast out of the heavenly council at Calvary, and finally bound on earth. “The question sometimes arises, ‘What will take place during the thousand years between the binding of Satan and his being loosed for a little season?’ To John was revealed the event which would take place during that time” (SSP 328.1).

Haskell quotes Isa 24:21-22 as a structural parallel beside Rev 20:1-3: “And they shall be gathered together, as prisoners are gathered in the pit, and shall be shut up in the prison, and after many days shall they be visited” (Isa 24:21-22, quoted at SSP 328.2 margin). He titles this section “third fall of satan,” reading the descending angel of Rev 20:1 as the executor of Satan’s final dispossession: “It does not take Christ or a host of angels to overcome Satan now, one angel” can do it (SSP 328.2). The key and chain are emblems of judicial authority, not literal hardware—a powerful angel rather than Christ Himself, since the saints have already gone up to the heavenly New Jerusalem (SSP 327.1).

Loughborough frames the descending angel of v.1 against a polemical backdrop—the destruction of the “temporal millennium” / Whitbyan scheme that placed Christ’s reign on a converted earth before his second advent. Citing Patterson and surveying the apostate “Christless millennium” that the Reformers had “instantly swept away” (GSAM 74.2), Loughborough recovers the pre-Reformation reading: Christ returns first; then the binding scene of Rev 20:1 takes place.

“The promises of his second coming and reign on earth were interpreted to mean simply the spread of his gospel, and the submission of a great part of the world to Christianity for a period of a thousand… years… This theory was elaborated and popularized by an English commentator named Whitby” (GSAM 75.3). Against this, Loughborough preserves the Millerite/Sabbatarian-Adventist reading: a literal descending angel, a real binding, a real desolated earth, a real second advent before the events of Rev 20. The chain-and-key are not a converted civilization, they are the post-Advent shackling of a defeated adversary.

Fitch’s 1841 Letter to Litch is one of the earliest Millerite statements of the pre-millennial / two-resurrection framework that locates the descending angel of Rev 20:1 between Christ’s personal second advent and the new-earth state. “I believe that the two resurrections, spoken of in the twentieth chapter of the Revelation, will take place literally, as there laid down… This thousand years I believe to be the millennium, and the only millennium which has ever been promised to the church” (LJL 14.2).

Fitch’s strict literalism on the chapter sets the rule by which the descending-angel scene is read: “I take the plainly revealed word of God, in this chapter, for truth, and know of no reason for doing otherwise… I do not find one text of Scripture, nor one shadow of an argument” for treating the resurrections as mystical (LJL 14.3). The angel is real, the chain is figurative-for-real-restraint, and the “bottomless pit” is a real period of confinement; the figurativeness of the chain does not dissolve the literal historical sequence Fitch is defending against Whitbyan spiritualism.

modernReadings.html: Same identification — a powerful angel (not specified as Christ) descends to bind Satan after the time of trouble. Verbatim: "**at the end of the time of trouble, a powerful angel will come down from heaven with the key to the bottomless pit and a chain in his hand to bind Satan for one thousand years. Having lost his followers to death, Satan will be powerless**" (p. 262). On the "bottomless pit" / _abussos_: "**The translation 'bottomless pit' in Revelation 20:1 is unfortunate. There is nothing literally bottomless about the pit. The Greek word is abussos from where we get the English word 'abyss'… the Greek translation (LXX) of the Old Testament word 'deep' (tehom) is the same as the one in Revelation 20:1 (abussos)**" (pp. 262-263). And the Gen 1:2 / Rev 20:1 cross-link: "**We must link Revelation 20:1 with Genesis 1:2 (in the LXX) because the same Greek word is used to describe the chaotic condition of the earth before creation in Genesis and after the seven plagues in Revelation**" (p. 262). Bohr also stacks the scapegoat / Lev 16:21-22 typology at p. 265: "**The punishment of Satan reminds us of the fate of the scapegoat on the Day of Atonement. The scapegoat was bound by a fit man and then taken to the wilderness where there were no inhabitants**." Fully convergent with Smith on every point — scapegoat typology, abyss = desolate-earth chaos, angel ≠ Christ.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Exemplary Rule V (Gen 1:2 LXX _abussos_ = Rev 20:1 _abussos_, Scripture as its own expositor for the symbol). Rule XIII typology-must-match-chronology via Lev 16:21-22 scapegoat-into-uninhabited-land = Satan-into-desolate-earth. Convergent.

verses.text: And he laid hold on the dragon, that old serpent, which is the Devil, and Satan, and bound him a thousand years,

pioneerReadings.html:
EGW gives the classic Adventist chain-of-circumstances reading: Satan is “bound” not by a literal chain but because there are no subjects left to deceive. “Here is to be the home of Satan with his evil angels for a thousand years. Limited to the earth, he will not have access to other worlds to tempt and annoy those who have never fallen. It is in this sense that he is bound: there are none remaining, upon whom he can exercise his power. He is wholly cut off from the work of deception and ruin which for so many centuries has been his sole delight” (GC 659.1).

The earth itself becomes the prison: “Here he will be confined, to wander up and down over the broken surface of the earth and see the effects of his rebellion against God’s law. For a thousand years he can enjoy the fruit of the curse which he has caused” (EW 290.1). The “old serpent” / “devil” / “Satan” quadruple naming of v.2 EGW reads as the gathering of every name the great controversy has hung on him—all of his guilt is now visible because all of his power-base is dead and his sphere is reduced to a ruined planet.

**The "binding" is by circumstance — no subjects left to work on.** Verbatim: "**We well know that Satan, in order to work, must have subjects upon whom to work. Without these, he can do nothing. But during the thousand years of his confinement to this earth, all the saints are in heaven, beyond the power of his temptations; and all the wicked are in their graves, beyond his power to deceive. His sphere of action is circumscribed, he being at this time confined to this earth; and thus is he bound, being condemned throughout this period to a state of hopeless inactivity**" (DAR 691.1). Smith answers the literalist objection directly: "**how often do we hear, in the daily transactions of life, such expressions as these: My way was completely hedged up; my hands were completely tied, etc. But do we understand, when persons use such expressions, that some insurmountable obstacle was literally thrown across the path they were traveling, or that their hands were literally confined with ropes or cords? — No; but simply that a combination of circumstances rendered it impossible for them to act**" (DAR 691.2). On Satan's spatial confinement: "**He no longer has the power of traversing space, and visiting other worlds; but like man he is confined to this earth, which he nevermore leaves. The place of the ruin he has wrought now becomes his gloomy prison-house, till he is led out to execution, at the end of the thousand years**" (DAR 692.1).

Litch reads the binding as one of five great deliverances secured at the Second Advent. “They shall be delivered from the temptations of the devil, who shall be chained and cast into the bottomless pit, and be shut up until the final resurrection; and even then can have no power on those who have part in the first resurrection” (PREX2 117.2).

The “chain” for Litch functions as the cessation of Satanic temptation, not a literal fetter; its scope is exactly the 1000-year interval between the two resurrections, with the resurrection-of-life class permanently immune to anything Satan can do post-release. Litch’s framing is restrained and convergent with later SDA exposition: the binding is the Second-Advent destruction of Satan’s power-base, and the immunity of the first-resurrection class anchors the figurative reading.

Crosier links the binding to the Matt 12:29 binding-of-the-strong-man saying within the Lev 16 scapegoat antitype. “The ‘strong man armed’ will have been bound by a stronger than he, ‘and his house (the grave) spoiled of its goods (the saints).’ Matthew 12:29… The thousand years imprisonment of Satan will have begun, and the saints will have entered upon their millennial reign with Christ” (SANC 24.2).

For Crosier the binding of v.2 is the culmination of the heavenly-sanctuary cleansing he had argued at length: with the antitypical Day of Atonement complete, the High Priest has finished His ministry, the sins have been transferred to the antitypical scapegoat (the “author of sins” receiving them back), and the “old serpent” / Devil / Satan named in v.2 is the very being who is bound. The mechanism is the spoiling of the grave at the first resurrection (Matt 12:29)—Christ has rescued His own; Satan now has none to work upon.

Haskell’s reading of the binding is built on Satan’s power-base collapse at the Second Coming. Before the Advent: “Since the resurrection of Christ, Satan, knowing that his time for work was short, has put forth all his strength in gaining subjects for his kingdom. He goes about to-day as a roaring lion, seeking whom he may devour. The kingdoms of the earth are becoming more and more under his power” (SSP 327.1). Then the Advent strips that power-base away: “The wicked are slain by the brightness of His coming, and are scattered over the face of the earth—a feast for birds” (SSP 327.1).

With every subject dead in the grave and every saint translated to the heavenly city, the “binding” reduces to a fact-of-circumstance: Satan has no one left to work on. Haskell quotes Isa 24:21-22 as the parallel-text: the “prisoners” gathered in the pit and shut up “after many days shall they be visited” (SSP 328.2 margin)—the “visit” being the second resurrection at the end of the thousand years. Convergent with Smith on every point.

Fitch grounds the binding of Satan in the strict literal reading of the chapter as a whole. “I believe that the two resurrections, spoken of in the twentieth chapter of the Revelation, will take place literally, as there laid down… This thousand years I believe to be the millennium, and the only millennium which has ever been promised to the church” (LJL 14.2).

Fitch reads the chapter sequentially and refuses spiritualizing solvents: “I do not find one text of Scripture, nor one shadow of an argument” for treating the first resurrection as mystical (LJL 14.3). The same hermeneutic governs the binding: real dragon, real 1000-year period, real “rest of the dead” class not raised until after. The mechanism (Satan’s power-base destroyed at the Advent) is implicit in the resurrection-of-the-wicked-only-at-the-end framework Fitch defends throughout the letter.

modernReadings.html: **Same chain-of-circumstances reading, framed as the captivity-by-loss-of-power-base argument paralleled to the papacy's deadly wound of Rev 13:9-10.** Verbatim: "**During the 1260 years, the papacy used the power of the kings of Europe to accomplish her purposes. However, at the end of this period, the papacy received a deadly wound when the kings of Europe withdrew their support. This withdrawal of support is referred to as the papacy going into captivity (Revelation 13:9, 10). That is to say, when the papacy had the support of the kings of Europe, she had power and was free. However, when the kings no longer supported her, she was bound and in captivity**" (pp. 270-271). Application to Satan: "**Something similar will happen to Satan during the millennium. During the time of trouble, Satan will be free and powerful because he has the support of the civil powers of the world to implement his agenda. However, at the second coming Satan's power base will all die and as a result he will be in captivity. In other words, Satan is loose when his power base is alive, he is bound when his power base is dead, and he will be loose again when his power base resurrects**" (p. 271). And: "**Thus, Revelation 20:5 contains the key that explains the binding and unbinding of Satan**" (p. 271). Fully convergent with Smith — the binding is non-literal, by loss of any one to tempt.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Rule V (the Rev 13:9-10 captivity / Rev 20:2 binding cross-link via shared diction; the Rev 20:5 second-resurrection / Rev 20:7 release link explains the mechanism). Rule XI literal-first respected by both — both expositors note that "bound" with a "chain" cannot be taken hyperliterally given a being who is a spirit (Eph 6:12); the non-literal reading is forced by the nature of the subject, not imposed by hermeneutic preference. Convergent.

verses.text: And cast him into the bottomless pit, and shut him up, and set a seal upon him, that he should deceive the nations no more, till the thousand years should be fulfilled: and after that he must be loosed a little season.

pioneerReadings.html:
EGW identifies the “bottomless pit” with the desolated earth post-plagues and post-Advent. “At the coming of Christ the wicked are blotted from the face of the whole earth—consumed with the spirit of His mouth and destroyed by the brightness of His glory. Christ takes His people to the City of God, and the earth is emptied of its inhabitants. ‘Behold, the Lord maketh the earth empty, and maketh it waste, and turneth it upside down’… Isaiah 24:1, 3, 5, 6” (GC 657.1).

The earth post-Advent: “The earth looked like a desolate wilderness. Cities and villages, shaken down by the earthquake, lay in heaps. Mountains had been moved out of their places, leaving large caverns. Ragged rocks, thrown out by the sea, or torn out of the earth itself, were scattered all over its surface… The wrath of God in the seven last plagues had been visited upon the inhabitants of the earth… The earth seemed to be deluged with blood” (EW 289.3-290.1).

The seal upon Satan, the no-more-deceiving, and the “little season” loosing are all rooted by EGW in the Isa 24 / Jer 4 chaos-language: the planet itself becomes the abyss in which the dragon is shut up.

**The "bottomless pit" = the desolate earth in chaotic / pre-creation state.** Verbatim: "**The original word signifies an abyss, bottomless, deep, profound. Its use seems to be such as to show that the word denotes any place of darkness, desolation, and death… the passage which specially throws light upon the meaning of the word here is Genesis 1:2, where we read that 'darkness was upon the face of the deep.' The word there rendered deep is the same word that is here rendered bottomless pit; so that passage might have been translated, 'Darkness was upon the face of the abyss, or bottomless pit.' But we all know what is meant by the word deep as there used; it is applied to this earth in its chaotic state. Precisely this it must mean in this third verse of Revelation 20**" (DAR 690.3). And on the earth's post-Second-Coming condition: "**At this time, let it be borne in mind, the earth is a vast charnel-house of desolation and death. The voice of God has shaken it to its foundations; the islands and mountains have been moved out of their places; the great earthquake has leveled to the earth the mightiest works of man; the seven last plagues have left their all-desolating footprints over the earth; the burning glory attending the coming of the Son of man has borne its part in accomplishing the general desolation; the wicked have been given to the slaughter, and their putrefying flesh and bleaching bones lie unburied, ungathered, and unlamented from one end of the earth to the other end thereof. Thus is the earth made empty and waste, and turned upside down. Isaiah 24:1. Thus is it brought back again, partially, at least, to its original state of confusion and chaos. (See Jeremiah 4:19-26, especially verse 23.) And what better term could be used to describe the earth thus rolling on in its course of darkness and desolation for a thousand years than that of the abyss, or bottomless pit?**" (DAR 690.3).

Miller takes verse 3 in its “simple, plain meaning; no mystery in this.” He reads the bottomless pit as “hell” (cross-referencing Rev 1:18 — “I am he that liveth and was dead… and have the keys of hell and of death”) and treats the sealing as a literal restraint, not a moral metaphor.

His hermeneutic on the thousand years is the decisive move: “If the thousand years had been used, in this chapter, or any where else in the word of God, in a mystical or figurative sense, it would have been somewhere explained; but, as it is not, I consider we are to place upon it the most simple construction, and I shall therefore understand it literally” (MWV2 30.1).

The “little season” release after the millennium is for him equally literal — the staging ground for the second resurrection of v.5 and the Gog-Magog gathering of v.7-9. Miller’s entire premillennial system hangs on this literalist rule, and verse 3 is the first hinge: a binding with a real end-date, after which Satan really walks out for a real little season.

Litch reads verse 3 as the legal ground of the saints’ millennial deliverance. In his enumeration of what the redeemed are delivered from at Christ’s coming, the third item is precisely the binding of v.3: “They shall be delivered from the temptations of the devil, who shall be chained and cast into the bottomless pit, and be shut up until the final resurrection; and even then can have no power on those who have part in the first resurrection” (PREX2 117.2).

Two exegetical moves stand out. First, the bottomless pit is a real prison with a real seal, not a symbol of moral inactivity. Second, the “little season” release at the end has a hard limit: Satan’s post-millennial loosing gives him no purchase on those raised in the first resurrection, only on the wicked dead now raised to face judgment.

Litch’s framing therefore reverses the postmillennial reading: the binding is not the inauguration of a golden age on earth, but the executive removal of the deceiver while the saints reign with Christ — and the loosing is not a tragedy but the final exposure of unregenerate hearts that proves the justice of the second-death sentence.

James White (quoting Miller’s narrative) anchors verse 3 to the only millennium Scripture teaches. “The only millennium taught in the word of God is the thousand years which are to intervene between the first resurrection and that of the rest of the dead, as inculcated in the twentieth of Revelation; and that it must necessarily follow the personal coming of Christ and the regeneration of the earth” (SLWM 53.1, citing Rev 20:2-7).

The shutting-up of Satan in v.3 is therefore the inaugural act of that interval, not a symbolic inhibition of evil during a converted-world era. “Till Christ’s coming, and the end of the world, the righteous and wicked are to continue together on the earth, and… the horn of the papacy is to war against the saints until his appearing and kingdom, when it will be destroyed by the brightness of Christ’s coming; so that there can be no conversion of the world before the advent” (SLWM 53.1).

White’s use of verse 3 is structural: the binding is the precondition of the millennium, not its result, and the “little season” release sets up the post-millennial judgment and lake of fire in vv.7-15 — the entire arc that Sabbatarians used to dismantle the temporal-millennium thesis.

Crosier sees in verse 3 the executive enactment of the Day-of-Atonement scapegoat ritual. The chaining and casting of Satan into the bottomless pit is the antitype of Leviticus 16:21-22 — the live goat sent “by the hand of a fit man into the wilderness… unto a land not inhabited.”

For Crosier, “a land not inhabited” is the desolate earth of the thousand years: the saints are removed to the heavenly Jerusalem, the wicked are dead, and Satan — bearing the confessed iniquities transferred from the sanctuary — is the only conscious being left to wander in the chaotic ruin. The sealing of the pit is the “shut up” clause of the wilderness exile.

Two payoffs follow. First, the binding is therefore not moral suppression — it is judicial banishment, the chain being “a great chain” of circumstances (no nations to deceive, no saints to assail). Second, the post-millennial loosing is not Satan’s rehabilitation but the moment the scapegoat’s sins return to consume him in the lake of fire of v.10, fulfilling Psalm 7:16: “His mischief shall return upon his own head.”

Haskell preserves the same desolated-earth = abyss identification within his three-falls-of-Satan structural framework. “The kingdoms of the earth are becoming more and more under his power… finally the Saviour will appear to take these to the city which He is now preparing for them. The wicked are slain by the brightness of His coming, and are scattered over the face of the earth—a feast for birds” (SSP 327.1).

The “seal” on Satan and the “no more deceiving the nations” clause Haskell illuminates by stacking Isa 24:21-22 against Rev 20:3 in his margin: “they shall be gathered together, as prisoners are gathered in the pit, and shall be shut up in the prison, and after many days shall they be visited” (SSP 328.2 margin). The “little season” / “after many days” is the second-resurrection window in which Satan recovers his deceivable subjects. Convergent with Smith on the abyss-as-ruined-earth, on the cross-link to Isa 24, and on the binding-by-loss-of-power-base mechanism.

Fitch reads verse 3 with iron literalism: the binding, the pit, and the thousand years are exactly what the text says. “I take the plainly revealed word of God, in this chapter, for truth, and know of no reason for doing otherwise; and because I find not one passage in all the Bible to support the idea of a millennium previous to the resurrection” (LJL 14.3).

His refusal to mysticize the binding is methodological: “If the resurrections spoken of in the twentieth chapter of the Revelation, are not to take place, literally, as therein laid down, then it is utterly impossible to prove from the Bible that there ever will be a resurrection… and so the Bible doctrine of a resurrection becomes a mere figure, and all vanishes into mysticism. Such opinions I discard” (LJL 14.3).

The implication for v.3 is direct: if the resurrection bookends of vv.4-5 are literal events, the binding of v.3 must be a literal removal of Satan from the field of operations. The “little season” of post-millennial release in v.3 then sets up the Gog-Magog scene of vv.7-9, which Fitch will likewise refuse to allegorize.

modernReadings.html: **Same identification — the desolate, de-created earth as the abyss in which Satan is shut up.** Verbatim: "**This word in Genesis describes a planet in a disorderly and empty state before creation week. In Revelation, just before the binding of Satan, the plagues of Revelation 16 will reverse creation and return the earth, in a great degree, to pre-creation chaos. The plagues will afflict the very things that God made during creation week. We might say that the plagues cause de-creation!**" (p. 263). Bohr stacks the de-creation effects (darkness, broken surface, heavenly bodies moved, vegetation scorched, seas to blood, fish/birds/humans dead — pp. 263-264), then cross-links Jer 4:19-26 (the prophet's vision of the earth "_without form and void_" matching Gen 1:2): "**I beheld the earth, and indeed it was without form, and void; and the heavens, they had no light**" (Jer 4:23, quoted p. 264). And on Satan's chained-in-the-abyss state: "**God will bind Satan on an earth that has returned to pre-creation disorder and emptiness. How could human beings live on the planet when the seas and fresh waters are all blood, the air has the stench of the dead, the planet is pitch dark because God has moved the heavenly bodies out of their places and there is no plant or animal life? The binding of Satan means that he cannot deceive the nations because there are no nations to deceive. However, after the millennium God will release Satan for a little while**" (p. 265). Fully convergent with Smith — Gen 1:2 / Jer 4:23 / Isa 24 backdrop for the abyss = desolate earth.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Exemplary Rule V (Gen 1:2 + Jer 4:23 + Isa 24:1-6 chain explains "bottomless pit" by Scripture itself). Rule IV (the chronology — Satan bound AFTER the close of probation, the seven plagues, the Second Coming, and the wicked's slaughter — is fixed by the convergence of Rev 16 + Rev 19:11-21 + Jer 25:30-38 + Isa 24). Convergent.

verses.text: And I saw thrones, and they sat upon them, and judgment was given unto them: and I saw the souls of them that were beheaded for the witness of Jesus, and for the word of God, and which had not worshipped the beast, neither his image, neither had received his mark upon their foreheads, or in their hands; and they lived and reigned with Christ a thousand years.

pioneerReadings.html:
EGW reads the thrones-and-judgment scene as the saints’ heavenly judicial work during the 1000 years, with 1 Cor 6 invoked explicitly. “Then I saw thrones, and Jesus and the redeemed saints sat upon them; and the saints reigned as kings and priests unto God. Christ, in union with His people, judged the wicked dead, comparing their acts with the statute book, the Word of God, and deciding every case according to the deeds done in the body. Then they meted out to the wicked the portion which they must suffer, according to their works; and it was written against their names in the book of death. Satan also and his angels were judged by Jesus and the saints” (EW 290.3).

The Pauline cross-link: “Satan also and evil angels are judged by Christ and His people. Says Paul: ‘Know ye not that we shall judge angels?’ Verse 3. And Jude declares that ‘the angels which kept not their first estate… He hath reserved in everlasting chains under darkness unto the judgment of the great day.’ Jude 6” (GC 661.1).

The locale is fixed to the heavenly New Jerusalem (EW 52.2 — “after they receive their harps, their robes, and their crowns, and enter the city, Jesus and the saints sit in judgment”). The “beheaded” class includes the martyrs of all ages; the no-mark class includes the last generation—the broader scope Smith defends grammatically.

**The exaltation of the saints in heaven, reigning with Christ — TWO classes singled out for special mention: the martyrs (beheaded for the witness of Jesus) AND those who refused the mark of the beast (the last generation).** Verbatim: "**From the Devil in his gloomy confinement, John now directs our attention to the saints in victory and glory, — the saints reigning with Christ — their employment being to assign to the wicked dead the punishment due their evil deeds. From that general assembly John then selects two classes as worthy of especial attention: first, the martyrs, those who had been beheaded for the witness of Jesus; and secondly, those who had not worshiped the beast and his image**" (DAR 692.2). Smith parses the Greek carefully: "**The word rendered which, in the expression, 'and which had not worshiped the beast,' etc., shows that there is another class introduced. The word is the compound relative, ὅστις (hostis), not merely the simple relative ὅς… 'Whosoever; whichsoever; any one who; anything which'… As one class, John saw the martyrs, and as another, he saw those who had not worshiped the beast and his image**" (DAR 692.2). Smith corrects the "all the last generation will be slain" misreading: "**these are not the ones who are beheaded for the witness of Jesus, as some who claim that the last generation of saints are all to he slain, would have us believe**" (DAR 692.2). The saints' employment is judicial: "**their employment being to assign to the wicked dead the punishment due their evil deeds**" (DAR 692.2).

Miller’s “Lecture 2: The First Resurrection” is built on Rev 20:4-6 and gives a careful exposition of the “blessed and holy” class who sit on the thrones. “Blessed and holy is he that hath part in the first resurrection; on such the second death hath no power; but they shall be priests of God and of Christ, and shall reign with him a thousand years” (MWV2 28.2).

The “blessed and holy” designation Miller traces through the prophets (Isa 30:18; Dan 12:12) as a settled identifier of the saints awaiting the Advent (MWV2 28.3). The reigning-as-priests function: “To be priests unto God and unto Christ, is to be holy; to be a kingdom of priests of a peculiar people, that should show forth his praises by declaring to the universe that out of nature’s darkness they had been redeemed by his blood, called by his grace unto his glorious, happy, and holy kingdom” (MWV2 35.1, citing Rev 5:9-10; 1:6; 1 Pet 2:5, 9).

Divergence flag: Miller’s pre-1844 lectures frame the saints reigning “on the earth” (MWV2 35.1) rather than in the heavenly New Jerusalem during the 1000 years—a residue of his initial “earth cleansed at the Advent” framework. The mature post-1844 Sabbatarian refinement (Crosier/J. White/Smith) locates the saints in heaven during the millennium and the city descending only after; on every other point—literal first resurrection, priestly judicial work, eternal reign post-millennium—Miller is convergent.

Litch grounds the saints’ thrones-and-reign in the Second-Advent deliverance into “the glorious and immortal likeness of Christ”. “They shall be delivered from pain and death, into the glorious and immortal likeness of Christ, and reign with him forever in the kingdom of God” (PREX2 117.2). The reign begins at the first resurrection and is the eternally-secured station of the “blessed and holy” class.

Litch’s key contribution is anchoring the two-class structure (martyrs / no-mark refusers) in Daniel 12’s resurrection-of-the-just framework: “The distinct testimony of Revelation 20th chapter, is, that there will be two resurrections; and that a thousand years will intervene between the first and final resurrection. The ‘blessed and holy’ have part in the first resurrection” (PREX2 117.4). The thrones-and-judgment scene is the natural setting in which that “blessed and holy” first-resurrection class exercises its priestly-judicial reign with Christ.

Crosier ties the thrones-and-reign scene of v.4 to the entry-into-millennial-reign that follows the completion of the antitypical Day of Atonement. “The thousand years imprisonment of Satan will have begun, and the saints will have entered upon their millennial reign with Christ” (SANC 24.2). The saints’ throne-sitting is the necessary corollary of the heavenly-sanctuary cleansing Crosier had argued: with the High Priest’s ministry complete and the sins transferred to the antitypical scapegoat, the redeemed are ushered into their kingly-priestly office (Rev 1:6 + 5:10 + 20:4 cross-stack).

Crosier does not separately exegete the beheaded class or the judicial mechanism, but his 1846 essay establishes the framework that makes the heavenly-locale reading possible: the saints reign with Christ in the sanctified-by-priesthood realm to which the High Priest’s ministry has cleansed the way.

Haskell reads the thrones-and-judgment scene as the saints’ judicial work in the New Jerusalem during the 1000 years. “‘And I saw thrones, and they sat upon them, and judgment was given unto them.’ During the thousand years, the saints live in the New Jerusalem, the city of God; and as priests of God and of Christ, they sit in judgment on the cases of the wicked. ‘Do ye not know,’ wrote Paul to the Corinthians, ‘that the saints shall judge the world? …Know ye not that we shall judge angels?’ Peter had this judicial work in mind when he wrote that ‘God” (SSP 329.1, citing 1 Cor 6 and 2 Pet 2:4).

The two-class martyr structure of v.4 Haskell preserves: the beheaded-for-the-witness class and the no-mark-of-the-beast refusers (margin quoting the verse in full at SSP 328.2). The heavenly locale is fixed: the saints “live in the New Jerusalem” during the entire 1000 years, with the judicial work as their primary employment. Convergent with Smith on locale, the two-class structure, and the 1 Cor 6 cross-link.

Jones reads v.4 within his sustained argument that Christ’s present priestly office is preparatory to the saints’ future priestly-kingly office during the 1000 years. “We must consider Him in His office as priest, not only that we may have the infinite benefit of His priesthood, but also that we shall be prepared for what we are to be. For it is written: ‘They shall be priests of God and of Christ, and shall reign with Him a thousand years.’ Revelation 20:6” (CWCP 8.3).

Jones extends the priesthood-and-reign motif into the sanctuary-doctrine context of Hebrews: the first chapter of Revelation shows Christ in priestly raiment, with the heavenly temple and altar in view, and “there it is promised and declared that they who have part in the first resurrection and upon whom the second death hath no power ‘shall be priests of God and of Christ, and shall reign with Him a thousand years’ in that priesthood” (CWCP 86.3). The throne-sitting and judicial work of v.4 is, for Jones, the inheritance of the present priestly preparation under Christ’s heavenly-sanctuary ministry. Convergent with Smith on priestly identity and on the post-Advent / pre-new-earth 1000-year scope.

Fitch reads the “beheaded” and “no-mark” clauses together as identifying the full first-resurrection class—not a martyrs-only subset. “It is said, I know, that if this first resurrection is literal, none but martyrs will then be raised. This is to me a strange position. (1.) All who have been beheaded for the witness of Jesus, and for the word of God, (2.) all who have not worshipped the beast or his image, or received his mark, are to live and reign with Christ a thousand years. This is the first resurrection” (LJL 15.1).

Fitch resolves the “mark of the beast” identification as the lust for any power that opposes Christ: “Mark of the beast then is evidently the lust of power. It seems plain to my mind, therefore, that those who do not worship the beast, or his image, are those who submit to Christ; and that those who do not wear the mark of the beast, are those who have put away all lust for power, and laid their entire being at the foot of the cross. I suppose, therefore, that all who have said we will have Christ to reign over us, will live and reign with Him a thousand years” (LJL 15.1). The thrones-and-reign of v.4 is open to all the saved, not restricted to literal beheading. Convergent with Smith’s broader scope.

modernReadings.html: **Same identification — the saints in heaven, reigning with Christ, executing the millennial investigative-judgment of the wicked.** Verbatim: "**During the thousand years the righteous will perform a work of judgment and reign with Jesus**" (p. 267). On who is judged: "**The question is this: Who are the righteous going to judge during the thousand years? Clearly, they will not judge the righteous because they are already in heaven. Moreover, they will not judge the holy angels because holy beings do not need to be judged. This must mean that they will judge Satan and his angels as well as the wicked who remained on earth during the thousand years**" (pp. 267-268). Bohr stacks 1 Cor 6:1-3 explicitly: "**Do you not know that the saints will judge the world? And if the world will be judged by you, are you unworthy to judge the smallest matters? Do you not know that we shall judge angels?**" (p. 268). Bohr glosses the beheaded-for-the-witness clause within the bracket of his quote: "**[these are the martyrs that died during the little time of trouble just before the close of probation—see Matthew 24:9]**" (p. 267) — a narrower identification than Smith, who reads the martyr-class diachronically (martyrs of all ages) and the no-mark class as a second category. On the locale: "**Jesus then takes His people to the Father's house in heaven**" (p. 267, citing John 14:1-3). Convergent on heavenly locale, judicial employment, and 1000-year reign.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Rule V (1 Cor 6:1-3 + Rev 20:4 cross-link — the saints judge the world and angels; the locale fixed by John 14:1-3 + 1 Thess 4:16-17). Minor textual difference: Smith reads the second relative-clause class as the last-generation no-mark refusers (broader); Bohr narrows the beheaded class to the end-time-tribulation martyrs only. Both fall within Rule V — both ground their reading in Greek-grammar attention to the text. Convergent on the doctrine; minor variance in scope of the martyr-class.

verses.text: But the rest of the dead lived not again until the thousand years were finished. This is the first resurrection.

pioneerReadings.html:
EGW reads v.5 as the two-resurrection hinge of the entire chapter, with the wicked rising only at the close of the 1000 years. “At the close of the thousand years, Christ again returns to the earth. He is accompanied by the host of the redeemed and attended by a retinue of angels. As He descends in terrific majesty He bids the wicked dead arise to receive their doom. They come forth, a mighty host, numberless as the sands of the sea. What a contrast to those who were raised at the first resurrection! The righteous were clothed with immortal youth and beauty. The wicked bear the traces of disease and death” (GC 662.1).

The vision-language is even sharper: “Then, in terrible, fearful majesty, Jesus called forth the wicked dead; and they came up with the same feeble, sickly bodies that went into the grave. What a spectacle! what a scene! At the first resurrection all came forth in immortal bloom; but at the second the marks of the curse are visible on all” (EW 292.1).

The parenthetical “this is the first resurrection” clause EGW reads as a marker that anchors the entire chapter’s two-stage chronology: righteous first, wicked second, the millennium between.

**The foundational proof-text for the two-resurrection doctrine.** Verbatim: "**'The rest of the dead lived not again till the thousand years were finished.' Whatever may be said to the contrary, no language could more plainly prove two resurrections; the first, a resurrection of the righteous at the commencement of the thousand years; and the second, that of the wicked at the end of that period. On such as have part in the first resurrection, the second death will have no power**" (DAR 693.4). Smith defends the textual integrity of the clause against critical excision: "**To avoid the doctrine of two resurrections, some claim that the passage, 'But the rest of the dead lived not again until the thousand years were finished,' is an interpolation, not found in the original, and hence not genuine. Even if this were so, it would not disprove the main proposition that the righteous dead are raised by themselves, in a 'first resurrection,' and that there is a second resurrection a thousand years later, in which all the wicked are brought from their graves. But the criticism is not true. All scholarship is against it. The Revised Version retains the passage**" (DAR 693.3). On the wicked's literal resurrection: "**The wicked who are raised at the end of the thousand years as really live again as they have once lived on the earth. To deny this is to do violence to this scripture**" (DAR 694.1).

Miller’s exposition of v.5 builds the two-resurrection doctrine from John 5:29, Dan 12:2, 1 Cor 15:23, and 1 Thess 4:16 as a converging witness. “The resurrection of the saints is first as it respects order and time. Wherever the word resurrection is used in connection with life or damnation, the one unto life always comes first; as in Daniel 12:2, ‘Some to everlasting life, and some to shame and everlasting contempt;’ John 5:29, ‘They that have done good, unto the resurrection of life; and they that have done evil, unto the resurrection of damnation’… 1 Thessalonians 4:16, ‘the dead in Christ shall rise first’” (MWV2 29.1).

Miller frames v.5 as the textual settlement of the dispute: “I believe the millennium is between the two resurrections and two judgments: the righteous and the wicked, the just and the unjust” (MWV1 35.2). And: “I understand that the judgment day will be a thousand years long. The righteous are raised and judged in the commencement of that day, the wicked in the end of that day” (MWV1 35.1). The “rest of the dead” clause is for Miller the textual hinge that splits the universal resurrection of Acts 24:15 into two stages 1000 years apart—a structural move that fully aligns with the Sabbatarian-Adventist refinement.

Litch makes Rev 20:5 the “distinct testimony” that resolves all the resurrection texts into the two-stage Adventist framework. “The distinct testimony of Revelation 20th chapter, is, that there will be two resurrections; and that a thousand years will intervene between the first and final resurrection. The ‘blessed and holy’ have part in the first resurrection. There are two orders only, ‘the just and unjust;’ ‘the resurrection of life,’ and ‘the resurrection of damnation.’ The resurrection of life is the first—for on those who have part in it the second death hath no power; while those who have not their names in the book of life, will be cast into the lake of fire—it will be the resurrection of damnation” (PREX2 117.4).

Litch is methodologically explicit: a single text (Dan 12:2) cannot decide the question, but the Rev 20 testimony resolves the apparent simultaneity-language by supplying the 1000-year gap. The “rest of the dead” clause is the textual warrant for splitting John 5:29 into two stages. Convergent with Smith on the two-resurrection doctrine.

James White rests the entire Sabbatarian-Adventist millennium framework on the v.5 parenthetical and its corollary first-resurrection-class-only reading. “I found that the only millennium taught in the word of God is the thousand years which are to intervene between the first resurrection and that of the rest of the dead, as inculcated in the twentieth of Revelation” (SLWM 53.1).

White appends footnoted Scripture chains that bracket v.5: the bodies of the wicked are reserved “in prison and damnation” through the 1000 years (citing John 5:29; Acts 24:15; 2 Pet 3:7, 10; Jude 14-15; Rev 20:3, 13-15 at SLWM 52.1). The text-critical question about the v.5a clause that Smith later defends, White treats as settled: he quotes from his sources without raising the interpolation challenge. The two-resurrection doctrine—the “first” at the personal advent, the “rest” a thousand years later—is for White the unique biblical millennium.

Haskell reads v.5 as the door through which the second-resurrection class enters at the close of the 1000 years. “‘Blessed and holy is he that hath part in the first resurrection: on such the second death hath no power… And when the thousand years are expired, Satan shall be loosed out of his prison’” (SSP 334.1, citing Rev 20:6-7). The parenthetical “rest of the dead lived not again until the thousand years were finished” for Haskell unlocks the chronology of vv. 7-9: Satan is loosed precisely because the second-resurrection class has returned to deceivable existence.

“At the voice of God, the earth gave up the dead, who have long slept in her bosom. ‘The sea gave up the dead which were in it; and death and the grave delivered up the dead which were in them.’ They rise to see the holy city” (SSP 334.2). The two-stage resurrection is the structural spine on which the rest of the chapter hangs; v.5b is the explicit marker that the “first resurrection” is a strict subset, not the totality. Convergent with Smith.

Fitch reads v.5 as the structural anchor of the entire chapter’s literalism. “This thousand years I believe to be the millennium, and the only millennium which has ever been promised to the church… I believe that if the resurrections spoken of in the twentieth chapter of the Revelation, are not to take place, literally, as therein laid down, then it is utterly impossible to prove from the Bible that there ever will be a resurrection; because the same arguments that would prove these resurrections mystical or figurative, would prove the same thing with equal force respecting any other passage of the Bible which speaks of a resurrection” (LJL 14.2-3).

Fitch’s appeal to John 5:28-29 supplies the eis-as-“at” Greek-grammar argument: “I find, on examination, that the Greek preposition, eis, which in this passage is twice translated, unto, might with equal propriety have been translated, at, and then the passage would have been plain, fully harmonizing with the twentieth chapter of Revelation” (LJL 17.1). The “rest of the dead” clause is the textual specification of John 5:29’s “resurrection of damnation” at the 1000-year terminus. Convergent with Smith.

modernReadings.html: **Same two-resurrection doctrine, with a punctuation-clarification argument resolving the King-James reading ambiguity.** Verbatim: "**All beings in this world have experienced a first birth and life. When the millennium begins, those who died in Christ will resurrect to their 'second life' never to die again. On the other hand, the wicked will resurrect to their 'second life' after the thousand years, the saints will judge them and then they will suffer second death**" (p. 269). On the KJV/NKJV punctuation problem: "**However, the translation of Revelation 20:5 in many Bible versions is problematic. As is well known, the ancient New Testament manuscripts did not have punctuation marks so the translators have added them where they think they belong. The King James and New King James translation of Revelation 20:4-6 is confusing because it leaves the impression that those who resurrect at the end of the thousand years will resurrect in the first resurrection**" (p. 269). The fix: "**This seeming problem is easily resolved by placing parentheses around the part of verse 5 that reads, 'the rest of the dead did not live again until the thousand years were finished'. This is not some Adventist solution to the problem. Many modern versions do just this, including the New International Version, Today's English Version, the New Living Translation, the New Revised Standard Version**" (pp. 269-270). And: "**Thus, Revelation 20:5 contains the key that explains the binding and unbinding of Satan**" (p. 271) — i.e., Satan is loose when his power-base is alive, bound when dead, loose again when resurrected. Fully convergent with Smith on the two-resurrection doctrine.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Exemplary Rule V (Rev 20:5 is its own self-expositor — the "_rest of the dead_" parenthetical reading is grounded in the syntax of the verse, not imposed from outside) and Rule IV (the two-resurrection chronology is fixed by John 5:28-29's two-class "_resurrection of life_" vs "_resurrection of damnation_" + 1 Thess 4:16-17 first-resurrection-at-Christ's-coming + Acts 24:15 "_resurrection of the dead, both of the just and unjust_"). Convergent.

verses.text: Blessed and holy is he that hath part in the first resurrection: on such the second death hath no power, but they shall be priests of God and of Christ, and shall reign with him a thousand years.

pioneerReadings.html:
EGW reads the “second death has no power” clause as the irrevocable security of the first-resurrection class against the final annihilation visited on the wicked. The redeemed receive the kingdom and the priestly office at the same instant: “Then I saw thrones, and Jesus and the redeemed saints sat upon them; and the saints reigned as kings and priests unto God” (EW 290.3).

At the millennial close, when fire devours the wicked, the redeemed observe in safety: “Satan is the root, his children are the branches. They are now consumed root and branch. They have died an everlasting death. They are never to have a resurrection” (EW 295.1). The first-resurrection class’s immunity to the second death is structural: they are inside the descended New Jerusalem, glorified, priestly, while the second-death fire consumes everyone outside.

The 1000-year reign EGW reads not as a static throne-sitting but as an active investigative-judgment-of-the-wicked (EW 52.2; GC 660.3-661.1) followed at its close by the descent of the city and the executive judgment.

Smith folds v.6 into the two-resurrection block above: "**On such as have part in the first resurrection, the second death will have no power. They can pass unharmed through the elements which destroy the wicked like chaff. They will be able to dwell with devouring fire and everlasting burnings (Isaiah 33:14, 15); they will be able to go forth and look upon the carcasses of the men who have transgressed against the Lord, as the quenchless fire and undying worm are preying upon them. Isaiah 66:24. The difference between the righteous and the wicked in this respect is seen again in the fact that while God is to the latter a consuming fire, he is to his people both a sun and a shield**" (DAR 693.4). The "_priests of God_" / "_reign with him_" clauses are not separately exegeted; the substance is the second-death-has-no-power immunity of the first-resurrection class.

Miller’s entire “Lecture 2: The First Resurrection” is an exposition of Rev 20:6. He opens with the verse as text (MWV2 28.1-2) and works each clause: “blessed and holy” (28.3); “first resurrection” (29.1); “second death hath no power” (built across the lecture); “priests of God and of Christ” (35.1); “reign with him a thousand years” (37.1-38.1).

The contrast with the present probationary state: “while in this state of mortality and trial, we are called to servants and ministers of Christ; but then, in the millennial blessedness, we shall be called priests of God and of Christ. You may ask, ‘Why this distinction in the language?’ I answer, There is a great difference between the kingdom of Christ, as it was established when Christ was here on earth, and the kingdom given up to God” (MWV2 38.1, citing 1 Cor 15:24).

Miller’s refusal of the postmillennial alternative is direct: “Where, in all the prophecies, can any one show me that the church will be blessed and holy, or happy and righteous… until he comes, that is, at Christ’s second appearance?” (MWV2 37.1). The mark of the chapter is Christ’s personal advent first, the saints’ reign second, the new earth third—the framework Sabbatarian-Adventism inherits and refines.

Litch reads verse 6 as the formal warrant for the saints’ five-fold millennial deliverance. Because the second death has no power on those in the first resurrection, the redeemed are guaranteed: “1. From the bondage to the Gentiles, under which the church is now placed… 2. From all the evils and imperfections of the fall… 3. From the temptations of the devil, who shall be chained… 4. From pain and death, into the glorious and immortal likeness of Christ… 5. From all the evils which are to come on the ungodly in the day of retribution” (PREX2 117.2).

The priest-and-king clause is therefore not aspirational but already-secured: “Even then can have no power on those who have part in the first resurrection” (PREX2 117.2). The post-millennial loosing of Satan in v.7 cannot reach across the threshold of v.6.

This is the structural payoff Litch builds on: verse 6 is the line that separates the two resurrections and gives the first one its irrevocable safety. The wicked of the second resurrection still face the lake of fire; the “blessed and holy” of the first are sealed by the executive beatitude itself.

James White uses verse 6 (via Miller’s narrative) to define the millennium itself. “The only millennium taught in the word of God is the thousand years which are to intervene between the first resurrection and that of the rest of the dead, as inculcated in the twentieth of Revelation; and that it must necessarily follow the personal coming of Christ and the regeneration of the earth” (SLWM 53.1).

The priest-king status of v.6 is therefore exercised after the personal advent, not before. White’s polemical use is sharp: he weaponizes v.6 against any temporal-millennium that places a converted world prior to the resurrection. “Till Christ’s coming, and the end of the world, the righteous and wicked are to continue together on the earth… so that there can be no conversion of the world before the advent” (SLWM 53.1).

The implication for v.6 is that the “blessed and holy” class is exclusively post-resurrection and post-advent. The priesthood is the priesthood of the glorified saints in the heavenly Jerusalem during the thousand years, not a present ecclesial office. This locates the verse within the Sabbatarian heavenly-locale refinement that distinguishes their reading from the inherited Millerite earth-locale.

Haskell treats verse 6 as the formal beatitude on the first-resurrection class. He pairs it with v.5 and reads it as the structural divider of the millennium: “Blessed and holy is he that hath part in the first resurrection: on such the second death hath no power… And when the thousand years are expired, Satan shall be loosed out of his prison” (SSP 334.1, quoting Rev 20:5-7).

The beatitude has executive force for him. “At the voice of God, the earth gave up the dead, who have long slept in her bosom… They rise to see the holy city come down from God out of heaven” (SSP 334.2). Those who are “blessed and holy” are the priests-and-kings already inside the city when it descends; the second-death immunity of v.6 is precisely the basis on which they survive the post-millennial fire of vv.9-15.

This is decisive against any spiritualizing reading. The priesthood of v.6 is not a present ecclesial status but an eschatological vocation. The thousand-year reign is exercised from inside the descended city, while the wicked dead lie in their graves awaiting the second resurrection. Verse 6 thus names the only class for whom the lake of fire holds no terror.

Jones reads Rev 20:6 as the eschatological fulfillment of the present priestly preparation under Christ’s heavenly-sanctuary ministry. “We must consider Him in His office as priest, not only that we may have the infinite benefit of His priesthood, but also that we shall be prepared for what we are to be. For it is written: ‘They shall be priests of God and of Christ, and shall reign with Him a thousand years.’ Revelation 20:6” (CWCP 8.3).

The 1000-year priesthood is, for Jones, the gravitational center of his Christology: “There it is promised and declared that they who have part in the first resurrection and upon whom the second death hath no power ‘shall be priests of God and of Christ, and shall reign with Him a thousand years’ in that priesthood. And when the first heaven and the first earth shall have passed away and there shall be found no place for them, and the new heaven and the new earth shall have been brought in… then, and not until then, is it declared of the city of God: ‘I saw no temple therein’” (CWCP 86.3). The 1000-year priestly reign is bracketed by the heavenly-sanctuary phase (now and through the millennium) and the no-temple eternal state (after); v.6’s “priests of God and of Christ” is a millennial-phase designation. Convergent with Smith on priesthood and on the 1000-year scope.

Loughborough invokes verse 6 in his anti-Whitby polemic to establish that the first resurrection is bodily and the millennium personal. Against Daniel Whitby’s spiritual-millennium thesis — that the “first resurrection” meant the revival of evangelical religion during a thousand-year converted-world era — Loughborough insists that the priest-king status of v.6 belongs only to those literally raised from the dead.

His framing: if the “first resurrection” can be allegorized into the revival of true religion, then the beatitude on those who “have part in” it becomes a generic statement about converted believers in any age — and the second-death-has-no-power immunity becomes a vague assurance rather than the eschatological seal of the first-resurrection class.

The structural point is decisive: verse 6 is the verse on which the entire spiritual-millennium reading collapses. Whitby’s system cannot account for a post-millennial Satan, a post-millennial Gog-Magog, or a post-millennial second resurrection — all of which require that v.6’s beatitude name a distinct class of literally-resurrected saints whose immunity is grounded in their inclusion in the first resurrection, not in their general piety.

Fitch quotes v.6 verbatim as the warrant for the blessedness, holiness, second-death-immunity, and 1000-year reign of the first-resurrection class. “I believe that ‘Blessed and holy is he that hath part in the first resurrection; on such the second death hath no power, but they shall be kings and priests of God, and of Christ, and shall reign with Him a thousand years’” (LJL 16.1).

Fitch’s rule for the verse is the rule for the whole chapter: take it literally as it stands. The “second death” immunity of the verse is for Fitch the textual proof that the wicked’s post-millennial destruction (vv. 14-15) is a real annihilation from which the first-resurrection class is permanently exempt—the conditional-immortality / no-eternal-torment doctrine in seed form. The priestly reign is open to all the redeemed, not a martyr-only subset (LJL 15.1).

modernReadings.html: **Same identification — the first resurrection is of the righteous at the Second Coming; they are immune to the second death; they reign as priests with Christ 1000 years.** Verbatim: "**Revelation 20:6 describes the resurrection of the righteous at the second coming as the first resurrection: 'Blessed and holy is he who has part in the first resurrection. Over such the second death has no power, but they shall be priests of God and of Christ, and shall reign with Him a thousand years'**" (p. 267). And: "**The righteous who resurrect in the first resurrection will not be subject to the second death after the thousand years**" (p. 269). Convergent.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Rule V (the Rev 2:11 + Rev 20:6 + Rev 20:14 "_second death_" chain is self-expositing; the "_priests of God_" cross-links Rev 1:6 + Rev 5:10 — the same kingdom-and-priests language). Convergent.

verses.text: And when the thousand years are expired, Satan shall be loosed out of his prison,

pioneerReadings.html:
EGW reads the loosing of Satan as the inevitable corollary of the second resurrection: when the wicked rise, Satan recovers his power-base. “Now Satan prepares for a last mighty struggle for the supremacy. While deprived of his power and cut off from his work of deception, the prince of evil was miserable and dejected; but as the wicked dead are raised and he sees the vast multitudes upon his side, his hopes revive, and he determines not to yield the great controversy” (GC 663.1).

The vision-language: “Then I saw that Satan again commenced his work. He passed around among his subjects, and made the weak and feeble strong, and told them that he and his angels were powerful. He pointed to the countless millions who had been raised. There were mighty warriors and kings who were well skilled in battle… As they come forth from their graves, they resume the current of their thoughts where it ceased in death. They possess the same desire to conquer which ruled when they fell” (EW 293.1).

The “little season” / “loosed… out of his prison” clause is precisely the post-second-resurrection deception interval. Satan claims to be the prince who rescued them from death (GC 663.1)—the final masquerade before fire from God.

Smith treats v.7 in conjunction with vv. 8-10, but the loosing-mechanism is the second resurrection (the wicked rise; Satan now has subjects again). Implicit in DAR 691.2: "**there is here a great limitation of Satan's power, which may well be called a 'binding.' He no longer has the power of traversing space, and visiting other worlds; but like man he is confined to this earth, which he nevermore leaves**" — i.e., the binding ends when his power-base returns. Smith's whole vv. 7-10 block at DAR 694.3-696.2 frames the loosing as the prelude to the New-Jerusalem assault: "**At the end of the one thousand years, the holy city, the New Jerusalem, in which the saints have dwelt in heaven during that period, comes down, and is located upon the earth, and becomes the camp of the saints, around which the resurrected wicked come up, numberless as the sand of the sea. The Devil deceives them, and thus brings them up to this battle**" (DAR 694.3).

Miller reads verse 7 as the literal post-millennial release of Satan to gather the wicked dead. “When the thousand years are expired, Satan shall be loosed out of his prison… We may reasonably expect that, when Satan is let loose, all the damned spirits are let loose with him; and it has been strongly implied they were to live again in the body, at the end of the thousand years” (MWV2 32.1).

This connects verse 7 directly to the second resurrection introduced in v.5b: “The rest of the dead means the wicked dead, who do not have part in the first resurrection; lived not again, showing conclusively that it is a natural life and death spoken of” (MWV2 31.1).

The hermeneutic principle is fully literal: the “little season” is real time, not metaphor; the prison is real spatial confinement, not a moral state. Miller’s framework here is one of the cleanest expressions of two-resurrection premillennialism in pre-1844 Adventist literature: a thousand-year gap between the resurrection of the righteous (v.4-6) and the loosing-plus-raising of the wicked (v.7-13). Note the Sabbatarian refinement still to come: Miller pictures the saints reigning on earth, while the post-1844 Sabbatarians relocate them to the heavenly Jerusalem until v.9.

Litch acknowledges the post-millennial loosing but immediately neutralizes its threat to the redeemed. His five-fold deliverance schema already covers this verse: “They shall be delivered from the temptations of the devil, who shall be chained and cast into the bottomless pit, and be shut up until the final resurrection; and even then can have no power on those who have part in the first resurrection” (PREX2 117.2).

This is the structural point: the loosing of v.7 is a real release, but its reach is bounded. Satan recovers operational scope only over the wicked dead now raised in the second resurrection. The first-resurrection saints — the “blessed and holy” of v.6 — sit beyond his reach, sealed by their immunity to the second death.

The exegetical move is to read v.7 as the executive trigger for the post-millennial judgment scene of vv.8-15, not as a tragic re-entry of evil. Satan is loosed precisely to be exposed and destroyed: the loosing manifests the unchanged hatred of the wicked, vindicates the divine sentence, and inaugurates the second-death executive action. Litch’s framing keeps the chapter’s moral economy intact — deliverance for the saints, terminal sentence for the rebels.

Andrews reads verse 7 as the chronological hinge between the two phases of the third-angel penalty. “The seven last plagues are poured out on the living wicked; but the second part of the penalty… is not inflicted until the end of the thousand years, when all the wicked are raised and suffer together” (TMR 124.2).

The loosing of Satan in v.7 is therefore the procedural opening of that second phase. Andrews chains this directly to vv.8-9 in his exposition: “And when the thousand years are expired, Satan shall be loosed out of his prison, and shall go out to deceive the nations which are in the four quarters of the earth, Gog and Magog, to gather them together to battle… and fire came down from God out of heaven and devoured them” (TMR 124.4, quoting Rev 20:7-9).

This shows the Sabbatarian post-1844 framework in operation: the wicked are dead during the millennium, not living through it under spiritualized rule. The loosing of v.7 is the post-millennial release of Satan to operate on the resurrected second-resurrection class — not a release into a still-inhabited present world. Andrews’s use of Isa 34 and Mal 4 throughout makes the framework explicitly conditionalist: the loosing leads to the final conflagration, not eternal torment.

Haskell’s reading: the second resurrection is the unbinding mechanism. “‘Blessed and holy is he that hath part in the first resurrection: on such the second death hath no power… And when the thousand years are expired, Satan shall be loosed out of his prison.’ At the voice of God, the earth gave up the dead, who have long slept in her bosom. ‘The sea gave up the dead which were in it; and death and the grave delivered up the dead which were in them.’ They rise to see the holy city” (SSP 334.1-2).

For Haskell, “Satan shall be loosed out of his prison” in v.7 and “the earth gave up the dead” in v.13 are the same event read from two angles. The “prison” reduces precisely when the deceivable subjects return; the loosing is therefore not a fresh act of God on Satan but the lifting of the circumstance-of-loss-of-power-base. The Lev 16 scapegoat margin Haskell stacks against the loosing scene (margin at SSP 334.2) underlines the irony: the scapegoat’s “land not inhabited” period ends only when the wicked’s mass-grave is opened. Convergent with Smith.

Fitch’s explicit identification: Gog and Magog of v.7-8 = the “rest of the dead” of v.5b, i.e., the resurrected wicked of all ages. “I believe that ‘when the thousand years are expired, Satan shall be loosed out of his prison, and shall go out to deceive the nations, Gog and Magog, in the four quarters of the earth.’ I believe this Gog and Magog to be ‘the rest of the dead,’ who ‘live not again until the thousand years are finished’—that is, all the wicked; that these will then go up on all the breadth of the earth; i. e., will then be raised from the dead and compass the camp of the saints about, and the beloved city” (LJL 16.2).

The mechanism Fitch reads from v.5b → v.7: Satan’s “prison” ends when the wicked rise. The “nations” of v.7 are not modern geopolitical entities but the totality of the wicked dead now restored to deceivable existence. Convergent with Smith on the no-ethnic-Gog/Magog reading and on the second-resurrection-as-unbinding mechanism.

modernReadings.html: **Same — Satan loosed by the second resurrection giving him his power-base back.** Verbatim: "**At the end of the millennium God will release Satan from his prison: 'Now when the thousand years have expired, Satan will be released from his prison and will go out to deceive the nations which are in the four corners of the earth, Gog and Magog, to gather them together to battle, whose number is as the sand of the sea.'**" (p. 270). And the captivity-mechanism reapplied: "**Satan is loose when his power base is alive, he is bound when his power base is dead, and he will be loose again when his power base resurrects**" (p. 271). Bohr cross-links the pre/post-millennium parallel: "**Before the thousand years God's people will be in spiritual Jerusalem (Revelation 14:17-20) and after in literal Jerusalem (Revelation 20:7-9). Both before and after the cards will appear to be stacked against those who are in Jerusalem—the hordes of the wicked will be like the sand of the sea (Revelation 17:15; 20:8)**" (p. 271). Convergent with Smith.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Rule V (the Rev 20:5 second-resurrection → Rev 20:7 release mechanism is supplied by the text itself; the Rev 17:15 "_waters = peoples_" / Rev 20:8 "_as the sand of the sea_" multitude link is Bible-fixed-symbol Rule VII). Convergent.

verses.text: And shall go out to deceive the nations which are in the four quarters of the earth, Gog and Magog, to gather them together to battle: the number of whom is as the sand of the sea.

pioneerReadings.html:
EGW reads Gog and Magog as the totality of the resurrected wicked—not a geopolitical entity. The vast throng she describes by ages and types: “In that vast throng are multitudes of the long-lived race that existed before the Flood; men of lofty stature and giant intellect… There are kings and generals who conquered nations, valiant men who never lost a battle, proud, ambitious warriors whose approach made kingdoms tremble. In death these experienced no change. As they come up from the grave, they resume the current of their thoughts just where it ceased. They are actuated by the same desire to conquer that ruled them when they fell” (GC 664.1).

The marching order: “Then with Satan at their head, the multitude move on. Kings and warriors follow close after Satan, and the multitude follow after in companies. Each company has its leader, and order is observed as they march over the broken surface of the earth to the Holy City” (EW 293.2). The “four quarters of the earth” covers every place every wicked person ever died; the “sand of the sea” multitude diction matches the universal scope. No dispensational ethnic identification is entertained.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR 694.3-696.2, vv. 7-10 block — no separate v.8 paragraph
pioneerReadings.html: Smith treats the Gog-and-Magog assault as the resurrected wicked of all ages gathered under Satan against the New Jerusalem.
