From the vv. 7-10 block: "**the holy city, the New Jerusalem, in which the saints have dwelt in heaven during that period, comes down, and is located upon the earth, and becomes the camp of the saints, around which the resurrected wicked come up, numberless as the sand of the sea. The Devil deceives them, and thus brings them up to this battle. They are induced to commence an impious warfare upon the holy city, in prospect of some advantage to be gained by fighting against the saints. Satan doubtless persuades them that they can overcome the saints, dispossess them of their city, and still hold possession of the earth**" (DAR 694.3). Smith does not separately exegete "Gog and Magog" as a specific historical-ethnic identification — he reads it as the totality of the resurrected wicked.

Andrews quotes Rev 20:7-9 in full as the post-millennial scene in which Gog and Magog (the resurrected wicked) are gathered against the saints and consumed. “‘And when the thousand years are expired, Satan shall be loosed out of his prison, and shall go out to deceive the nations which are in the four quarters of the earth, Gog and Magog, to gather them together to battle; the number of whom is as the sand of the sea. And they went up on the breadth of the earth, and compassed the camp of the saints about, and the beloved city; and fire came down from God out of heaven and devoured them.’ Revelation 20:7-9” (TMR 124.4).

Andrews’s framing locates the v.8 multitude as the second-resurrection wicked, gathered for the executive judgment that climaxes vv. 9-15. His chapter on the third angel’s message warning—“The seven last plagues are poured out on the living wicked; but the second part of the penalty… is not inflicted until the end of the thousand years, when all the wicked are raised and suffer together” (TMR 124.2)—makes v.8’s gathering the assembly-for-final-judgment scene. No ethnic identification.

Haskell reads Gog and Magog as the gathered resurrected wicked of all ages, surrounding the descended New Jerusalem. “They rise to see the holy city” (SSP 334.2)—the same multitude described by Rev 20:13 (“the sea gave up the dead which were in it; and death and the grave delivered up the dead which were in them”).

Haskell does not assign Gog/Magog any modern ethnic identity. The “nations… in the four quarters of the earth” is for him simply the totality of the second-resurrection class; the “sand of the sea” diction is the same kind-of-multitude language used of Abraham’s seed (Gen 22:17). Convergent with Smith and against dispensational schemes.

Fitch’s identification: Gog and Magog of v.8 = “the rest of the dead” of v.5b = all the wicked. “I believe this Gog and Magog to be ‘the rest of the dead,’ who ‘live not again until the thousand years are finished’—that is, all the wicked; that these will then go up on all the breadth of the earth; i. e., will then be raised from the dead and compass the camp of the saints about, and the beloved city” (LJL 16.2).

Fitch’s rule of strict literalism for the chapter (LJL 17.1) settles the question against ethnic-Gog/Magog readings: the “nations” deceived in v.8 are not the present-day nation-states but the totality of the second-resurrection class. The “four quarters of the earth” locator language matches the universal scope of the resurrection from every grave. Convergent with Smith and against any dispensational identification.

modernReadings.html: **Same — Gog and Magog = the resurrected wicked of all ages, the multitude as the sand of the sea, surrounding the New Jerusalem.** Verbatim (quoting the verse with framing): "**Now when the thousand years have expired, Satan will be released from his prison and will go out to deceive the nations which are in the four corners of the earth, Gog and Magog, to gather them together to battle, whose number is as the sand of the sea**" (p. 270). The pre/post-millennium parallel: "**Both before and after the wicked will prepare for battle against the city (Revelation 16:12, 16; 20:7-9). Both before and after the battle never takes place**" (p. 271). Bohr does not give Gog/Magog a distinct ethnic identification either — the multitude designation is the resurrected wicked of all ages, matched to the "_sand of the sea_" diction.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Rule V (the Rev 16:12-16 / Rev 20:7-9 pre/post-millennium parallel is text-given). Rule VII for "_sand of the sea_" = innumerable multitude (cf. Gen 22:17; Heb 11:12). Notable: Bohr does NOT identify Gog/Magog with a modern geopolitical actor (Russia, the Islamic world, etc.) as dispensational schemes do — this is a key Rule-XI / Rule-V restraint shared with Smith. Convergent.

verses.text: And they went up on the breadth of the earth, and compassed the camp of the saints about, and the beloved city: and fire came down from God out of heaven, and devoured them.

pioneerReadings.html:
EGW reads v.9 as the climactic executive-judgment scene: the descended New Jerusalem is besieged, the wicked confronted with Christ in glory, then consumed by fire from God. “Jesus closes the gates of the city, and this vast army surround it, and place themselves in battle array, expecting a fierce conflict. Jesus and all the angelic host and all the saints, with the glittering crowns upon their heads, ascend to the top of the wall of the city. Jesus speaks with majesty, saying, ‘Behold, ye sinners, the reward of the just! And behold, My redeemed, the reward of the wicked!’” (EW 293.2).

The fire: “Satan rushes into the midst of his followers and tries to stir up the multitude to action. But fire from God out of heaven is rained upon them, and the great men, and mighty men, the noble, the poor and miserable, are all consumed together. I saw that some were quickly destroyed, while others suffered longer. They were punished according to the deeds done in the body” (EW 294.1).

The annihilation reading is anchored in Ezek 28: “I will bring thee to ashes upon the earth in the sight of all them that behold thee… Thou shalt be a terror, and never shalt thou be any more” (Ezek 28:18-19, quoted GC 672.1). And in Mal 4: “In the cleansing flames the wicked are at last destroyed, root and branch—Satan the root, his followers the branches” (GC 673.1).

**Fire from God = utter excision / annihilation.** Verbatim: "**But fire comes down from God out of heaven, and devours them. The word here rendered devoured, Professor Stuart admits is 'intensive,' and signifies, 'to eat up, devour, so that it denotes utter excision.' (Hudson's Christ our Life, p. 146.) This is the time of the perdition of ungodly men, — the time when the elements shall melt with fervent heat, the earth also, and when the works that are in the earth shall be burned up. 2 Peter 3:7, 10. In the light of these scriptures, we can see how the wicked are to receive their recompense in the earth (Proverbs 11:31); we can see also that this recompense is not eternal life in misery, but an 'utter excision,' entire and complete destruction**" (DAR 694.3). Smith explicitly rejects the millennial-saints-on-earth view and locks the New Jerusalem's descent as the trigger: "**The first is that the earth is renewed at the second coming of Christ, and is the habitation of the saints during the thousand years**" — refuted on the ground that the wicked would then "**come up, with the Devil at their head, and tread with their unhallowed feet upon the purified and holy earth, and the saints, who have held possession for a thousand years, are obliged to yield the ground, and flee into the city. But we cannot believe that the saints' inheritance will ever be thus marred**" (DAR 695.2).

Andrews quotes Rev 20:7-9 entire as the cap of his exposition of the third angel’s warning, then layers the Isa 34 conflagration prophecy beside it. “The prophet Isaiah (chap. 34) describes the final conflagration of our globe in language which is a complete parallel to that of the third angel in describing the punishment of the wicked… ‘For it is the day of the Lord’s vengeance, and the year of recompenses for the controversy of Zion. And the streams thereof shall be turned into pitch, and the dust thereof into brimstone, and the land thereof shall become burning pitch. It shall not be quenched night nor day; the smoke thereof shall go up forever’” (TMR 125.1-2).

The “fire from God” of v.9 Andrews reads as the final conflagration of the globe—the lake of fire in which the resurrected wicked, the false prophet, and the beast receive their reward (TMR 124.4). The locale is on earth, the time is post-millennial, the agency is divine fire, and the result—though “dreadful and long-continued” for each according to deserts (TMR 126.2)—is annihilation: “they shall die the second death… They shall perish, being consumed into smoke… they shall be as though they had not been” (TMR 126.2).

Haskell stages the v.9 fire-from-God as the convergence of fire from heaven and fire from the earth’s interior—the lake-of-fire scene synchronized with the city’s descent and the second-death annihilation. “Then from His throne, God breathes upon the assembled multitudes. Fire comes down from God out of heaven, and mingles with the fire which comes from the interior of the earth; and it devours them. ‘The devil that deceived them [the nations] was cast into the lake of fire and brimstone, where the beast and the false prophet are.’ ‘And death and hell were cast into the lake of fire… And whosoever was not found written in the Book of Life was cast into the lake of fire.’ This is the second death” (SSP 336.1).

The city in the midst of fire: “Here, the words of the Psalmist are fulfilled: ‘The righteous shall be recompensed in the earth: much more the wicked and the sinner.’ The city of God, like the ark in the flood, rides safely on the billows of flame” (SSP 336.1). The “compassed the camp of the saints… and the beloved city” clause of v.9 is the immediate setting for the post-millennial executive judgment, with the saints inside the descended city and the wicked outside. Convergent with Smith.

Waggoner uses verse 9 as the literal staging ground for the descent of the New Jerusalem. Tracing Abraham’s “heavenly country” promise (Heb 11:16) through to its eschatological fulfillment, he locates the millennial reign inside the city before its descent: “In the twenty-first first chapter of Revelation we find a description of those foundations, where we also find that the city will not always remain in heaven, but will descend to this earth with the saints who have reigned in it with Christ for a thousand years after the resurrection” (EVCO 424.4, citing Rev 20).

This is the explicit Sabbatarian inversion of the postmillennialist sequence. The saints reign in the heavenly city during the thousand years, then return to earth in the city at v.9 — where the compass-the-camp-of-the-saints language presupposes the city is already on earth, surrounded by the resurrected wicked of v.7-8.

Waggoner’s framing therefore puts the “beloved city” of v.9 = the descended New Jerusalem of Rev 21:2, and the “camp of the saints” = the saints stationed around or within the city as the wicked army assembles. The verse is the geographic intersection point where heavenly locale (saints during the millennium) meets earthly locale (post-millennial executive judgment).

Fitch reads the fire-from-God of v.9 as the agency of the second-death annihilation. “that these will then go up on all the breadth of the earth… and compass the camp of the saints about, and the beloved city; that fire will come down from God out of heaven and devour them; that the devil that deceived them will be cast into the lake of fire, where the beast and the false prophet are, and will be tormented day and night forever and ever; and that this is the second death” (LJL 16.2).

Fitch holds the literal sequence: the wicked surround the city, the fire descends, the destruction is the second death. The “camp of the saints” and “beloved city” on the breadth of the earth presume the descent of the New Jerusalem before the encirclement—the same Rev 21:2-before-Rev 21:1 chronological inversion Bohr later makes explicit.

modernReadings.html: **Same — fire from God devours the wicked surrounding the New Jerusalem; the descent of the city is the trigger.** Verbatim: "**They went up on the breadth of the earth and surrounded the camp of the saints and the beloved city and fire came down from God out of heaven and devoured them. The devil, who deceived them, was cast into the lake of fire and brimstone where the beast and the false prophet are and they will be tormented day and night forever and ever**" (p. 271, quoting Rev 20:9-10). On the city's location at the time of the assault: "**Revelation 20:7-9 tells us that the New Jerusalem is on the earth when the wicked surround it. This means that the New Jerusalem must have descended from heaven to earth before the wicked surrounded it. This being the case, Revelation 21:2 occurs chronologically before Revelation 21:1**" (p. 277). Fully convergent with Smith — the saints have been in heaven during the 1000 years, the city descends after, the resurrected wicked surround the camp of the saints and the city, fire from God devours them.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Exemplary Rule V (Rev 20:9 "_fire came down from God_" + 2 Pet 3:7-13 + Mal 4:1, 3 + Isa 33:14-15 + Ezek 28:18-19 chain — the annihilation reading is forced by the consonance of all the texts on the end-of-the-wicked). Convergent.

verses.text: And the devil that deceived them was cast into the lake of fire and brimstone, where the beast and the false prophet are, and shall be tormented day and night forever and ever.

pioneerReadings.html:
EGW reads the v.10 casting of Satan into the lake of fire as the longest-duration suffering within the second-death scene, with the “tormented… forever and ever” clause limited to the duration of the subject’s existence in the fire.

“Satan and his angels suffered long. Satan bore not only the weight and punishment of his own sins, but also of the sins of the redeemed host, which had been placed upon him; and he must also suffer for the ruin of souls which he had caused. Then I saw that Satan and all the wicked host were consumed, and the justice of God was satisfied” (EW 294.2). And: “Satan is the root, his children are the branches. They are now consumed root and branch. They have died an everlasting death. They are never to have a resurrection” (EW 295.1).

The scapegoat-Satan typology resolves at this point: “The sins of the righteous having been transferred to Satan, he is made to suffer not only for his own rebellion, but for all the sins which he has caused God’s people to commit. His punishment is to be far greater than that of those whom he has deceived. After all have perished who fell by his deceptions, he is still to live and suffer on. In the cleansing flames the wicked are at last destroyed, root and branch—Satan the root, his followers the branches” (GC 673.1). The lake of fire is the agency of final extinction, not eternal conscious torment.

**The beast and false prophet of Rev 19:20 are the ORGANIZATIONS, destroyed at the Second Coming; the individuals of those organizations come up in the Second Resurrection as Gog/Magog and are destroyed afresh.** Verbatim: "**It will be noticed that in the expression, 'where the beast and false prophet are,' are is a supplied word. It would be more proper to supply the words were cast, answering to what was spoken of the Devil just before. The sentence would then read, 'The Devil was cast into the lake of fire, where the beast and false prophet were cast.' The beast and false prophet were cast in there, and destroyed, at the commencement of the thousand years. Revelation 19:20. The individuals of whom those organizations were then composed, now come up in the second resurrection, and a similar and final destruction is visited upon them, under the names, Gog and Magog**" (DAR 696.3). Smith defines the lake of fire: "**As a comprehensive definition, may it not be called a symbol of the agencies which God employs to close up his controversy with the living wicked at the beginning of the thousand years, and with all the hosts of the ungodly at the end of that period? Literal fire will of course be largely employed in this work**" (DAR 697.1). On "_tormented day and night forever and ever_": Smith cross-refers his own Rev 14:11 treatment ("**see on chapter 14:12**") — the standard SDA reading is that _aiōnas tōn aiōnōn_ = the age-as-long-as-the-subject-lasts (cf. Jonah 2:6; Exod 21:6), not literal endless conscious torment.

Miller reads verse 10 as the final condemnation — “soul and body” — of the wicked. His paraphrase is uncompromising: “In this verse, the final condemnation of the wicked, soul and body, is given; and the last that God has seen fit to reveal concerning them to us is, that they are cast into everlasting torment” (MWV2 33.1).

This is the strongest moment of inherited language in Miller’s Rev 20 lecture: he retains the “everlasting torment” phrasing from the Authorized Version without explicit qualification. The Sabbatarian refinement will later sharpen this: Andrews, Haskell, and White read “tormented day and night for ever and ever” through Isa 34:10 and Mal 4:1 as duration-of-the-burning, not duration-of-consciousness, since the wicked are explicitly “consumed… root and branch” (Mal 4:1).

Divergence flag: Miller’s lecture leaves v.10 closer to inherited Protestant orthodoxy than to fully developed Adventist conditionalism. The structural premillennialism (two literal resurrections, literal binding) is fully in place; the conditionalist exegesis of the lake-of-fire language is not yet pressed. Note that even here Miller’s emphasis is sentence and condemnation, not gleeful eternal torture — he treats v.10 as the closing executive clause on the deceiver.

Litch reads verse 10 as the final-phase fulfillment of the saints’ fifth deliverance. The redeemed “shall be delivered from all the evils which are to come on the ungodly in the day of retribution” (PREX2 117.2). The casting of the devil into the lake of fire is precisely that day of retribution arriving on Satan and his confederate beast and false prophet.

The continuity with v.3 is structural: Satan was “chained and cast into the bottomless pit, and be shut up until the final resurrection,” and now in v.10 that final resurrection is over, the deceptive mission of v.8 has failed, and the deceiver is consigned to the same fate as those he deceived. The pit-prison of v.3 was custodial; the lake of fire of v.10 is executive.

Litch’s framing emphasizes the deliverance is symmetrical with the destruction: every threat that could have reached the first-resurrection saints — Satan’s temptation (v.3), his post-millennial deception (v.7-8), his armies’ assault on the camp (v.9), the lake of fire itself (v.10) — is neutralized by the second-death-has-no-power immunity granted in v.6. The verse is the final scene in the deliverance narrative, not a meditation on eternal sadism.

Andrews opens his v.10 exposition by quoting Rev 14:10-11 + Rev 20:10 together, then patiently establishes the annihilation reading against eternal-conscious-torment. “‘He shall be tormented with fire and brimstone in the presence of the holy angels, and in the presence of the Lamb; and the smoke of their torment ascendeth up forever and ever’… The final perdition of ungodly men in the lake of fire is without doubt the subject of these awful words” (TMR 124.3).

The four-point chain: (1) the punishment occurs on this earth, the final conflagration constituting the lake of fire (TMR 124.4); (2) Isaiah 34 supplies the parallel-text whose “smoke thereof shall go up forever” language has finite scope (TMR 125.1-2); (3) the duration is “dreadful and long-continued” but each is punished “according to his deserts” (TMR 126.2); (4) the result is total destruction: “They shall die the second death… They shall perish, being consumed into smoke… They shall be punished with everlasting destruction, being burned up in unquenchable fire… having been consumed, root and branch, they shall be as though they had not been” (TMR 126.2). The “tormented day and night forever and ever” of v.10 is read as the “everlasting result”—destruction with no resurrection, no remedy, no end. Fully convergent with Smith.

Haskell quotes vv. 10 and 14-15 together as a single death scene, with the city riding above the flames like Noah’s ark above the flood. “‘The devil that deceived them [the nations] was cast into the lake of fire and brimstone, where the beast and the false prophet are.’ ‘And death and hell were cast into the lake of fire… And whosoever was not found written in the Book of Life was cast into the lake of fire.’ This is the second death” (SSP 336.1).

“The city of God, like the ark in the flood, rides safely on the billows of flame. The elements melt with fervent heat, and the earth, with all its works is burned” (SSP 336.1). The beast and false prophet of Rev 19:20 are not still suffering in some other pit when Satan joins them in v.10—the scene gathers them all into one post-millennial lake-of-fire event. Convergent with Smith: lake of fire = annihilation, with the saints in the descended city untouched.

Fitch quotes v.10 verbatim within his post-millennial death-scene summary. “fire will come down from God out of heaven and devour them; that the devil that deceived them will be cast into the lake of fire, where the beast and the false prophet are, and will be tormented day and night forever and ever; and that this is the second death” (LJL 16.2).

Fitch’s identification of the “second death” with the casting-into-the-lake-of-fire is the seed of the conditional-immortality doctrine: the “tormented… forever and ever” clause is collapsed into the second-death clause, so the duration is the duration of the existence-in-the-fire, not endless conscious torment. This reading aligns with Smith on the v.14’s “this is the second death” gloss that defines what “forever and ever” here means.

modernReadings.html: **Same — annihilation reading, with explicit refusal to take "_tormented forever and ever_" as endless conscious torment.** From the v.10 quote at p. 271: "**The devil, who deceived them, was cast into the lake of fire and brimstone where the beast and the false prophet are and they will be tormented day and night forever and ever**." Bohr's cross-reference exposition at the volume's wider treatment (pp. 6029-6138): "**What is the meaning of the expression 'forever and ever'? Revelation 20:10 tells us…**" — Bohr establishes from _basanizo_ ("torment") + _aiōnas tōn aiōnōn_ that "**Scripture must be consistent with itself. Revelation 20:10 must then mean that…**" (p. 6138) — the wicked are tormented for the duration of their existence in the fire, not eternally. And: "**Revelation 20:10 cannot contradict Ezekiel 28:18, 19 or Malachi 4:1, 3 where**" the destruction is total and final (p. 6131). On the beast / false prophet identification in v.10: Bohr aligns with Smith (the beast and false prophet of Rev 19:20 = the apostate organizations cast in at the Second Coming; the lake doesn't fully ignite until after the millennium per GC 672-673, which Bohr cites at length in the volume's broader treatment). Fully convergent with Smith on annihilation, on the Rev 19:20 cross-link, and on the temporal-finite reading of "_forever and ever_".

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Rule IV (Ezek 28:18-19 + Mal 4:1, 3 + Rev 20:10 + Rev 20:14-15 + 2 Pet 3 + Isa 33 + Isa 34 chain — Scripture must be consistent with itself; the annihilation reading is the only one that satisfies all the texts). Rule V (the supplied-word _are_ → _were cast_ correction is internal to the verse's grammar). Convergent.

verses.text: And I saw a great white throne, and him that sat on it, from whose face the earth and the heaven fled away; and there was found no place for them.

pioneerReadings.html:
EGW reads the great white throne as the post-millennial executive-judgment throne from which the final coronation and sentence pronouncement issue. “In the presence of the assembled inhabitants of earth and heaven the final coronation of the Son of God takes place. And now, invested with supreme majesty and power, the King of kings pronounces sentence upon the rebels against His government and executes justice upon those who have transgressed His law and oppressed His people. Says the prophet of God: ‘I saw a great white throne, and Him that sat on it, from whose face the earth and the heaven fled away; and there was found no place for them. And I saw the dead, small and great, stand before God; and the books were opened: and another book was opened, which is the book of life: and the dead were judged out of those things which were written in the books, according to their works.’ Revelation 20:11, 12” (GC 666.1).

The “earth and heaven fled away” clause EGW reads in conjunction with 2 Pet 3:7-13 and Rev 21:1: the dissolution of the present heavens and earth makes way for the new-earth state. The scene is post-millennial, located on the earth (the descended New Jerusalem now stands on the renewed plain of EW 291.1).

**The great white throne = the post-millennial executive-judgment scene; "_earth and heaven fled away_" = the literal melting / dissolution of the earth's elements, the city resting on the molten globe like Noah's ark on the flood waters, the earth ultimately reduced to invisible gas before being re-created.** Verbatim: "**With verse 11, John introduces another scene to take place in connection with the final doom of the ungodly. It is the great white throne of judgment, before which they are assembled to receive their awful sentence of condemnation and death. Before this throne the heavens and the earth flee away, so that no place is found for them. A moment's reflection on the changes which must then take place in the earth will bring out the great force of this language. The scene is that of Peter's burning day, which is the 'perdition of ungodly men,' and in which even the 'elements' melt with fervent heat. 2 Peter 3:7-13**" (DAR 697.3). The progression: fire burns the wicked (DAR 698.1), then the earth fuses to molten mass with the city floating on it (DAR 698.2), then converts to gas (DAR 698.3), then re-creates as the new earth (DAR 699.1).

Miller treats verse 11 as the opening of a fresh vision of the same judgment day, viewed now from the throne side. “In the next verse, John has another vision of the same things which he had before told us, only in a difference point of view, or some circumstance not before clearly described. And I saw always implies a new view, or another vision” (MWV2 33.1).

Miller identifies the great white throne with Daniel’s vision: “This is the same throne that Daniel saw, 7:9-14… ‘I beheld till the thrones were cast down and the Ancient of days did sit, whose garment was white as snow, and the hair of his head like the pure wool: his throne was like the fiery flame, and his wheels as burning fire’” (MWV2 33.1, quoting Dan 7:9).

The structural payoff: verse 11 is the throne-side perspective on the entire judgment arc, beginning with Christ’s coming and the first resurrection (Dan 7:9-10 read with Rev 20:11-12) and culminating in the executive sentence on the wicked. Heaven and earth fleeing from God’s face is for Miller the literal eschatological dissolution presupposed by 2 Peter 3:10 — the same conflagration that supplies the “lake of fire” of vv.10, 14-15.

Andrews places verse 11 inside the post-millennial second-phase executive judgment. Because “the second part of the penalty… is not inflicted until the end of the thousand years, when all the wicked are raised and suffer together” (TMR 124.2), the great white throne is the bench from which that sentence falls.

His exegesis stays tight to the text: the throne scene must come after the loosing of Satan (v.7) and the gathering of Gog and Magog (v.8) but before or coincident with the fire from heaven (v.9). “And when the thousand years are expired, Satan shall be loosed out of his prison… and fire came down from God out of heaven and devoured them” (TMR 124.4, citing Rev 20:7-9).

Andrews’s framework therefore holds verse 11 is not the timeless homiletic backdrop of evangelistic appeal — it is the actual eschatological courtroom at the close of the millennium, the legal venue where the books of v.12 will be opened. Fleeing “heaven and earth” means the dissolution described in 2 Peter 3:10-13 (TMR 126.1) — the conflagration that prepares the new heavens and new earth.

Haskell collapses v.11’s “earth and heaven fled away” into the lake-of-fire annihilation event of vv. 9, 14-15. “The city of God, like the ark in the flood, rides safely on the billows of flame. The elements melt with fervent heat, and the earth, with all its works is burned” (SSP 336.1).

The fleeing of earth and heaven is the same event as the lake of fire’s consummation: the entire pre-new-earth physical order dissolves under the executive-judgment fire. The throne sits unmoved over the burning planet, the city rides above the flames, and the saints are inside.

Fitch’s literalist hermeneutic reads verse 11 as a real cosmic event, not a transitional figure. Because he refuses to mysticize the resurrections of vv.4-5 or the binding of v.3, the great white throne and the fleeing of heaven and earth must likewise be read as actual eschatological dissolution.

His operating principle: “I take the plainly revealed word of God, in this chapter, for truth, and know of no reason for doing otherwise… If the resurrections spoken of in the twentieth chapter of the Revelation, are not to take place, literally, as therein laid down, then it is utterly impossible to prove from the Bible that there ever will be a resurrection” (LJL 14.3).

By the same rule, the throne scene of v.11 is a forensic event, not a homiletic image. Fitch closes his letter by binding all of Rev 20 to this principle: “I believe these things because ‘thus saith the Lord,’ in the twentieth chapter of Revelation, and because I know of no shadow of a reason for calling them mysticisms. The opinion that they are mysticisms, I believe to be one of those groundless traditions of men, by which the commandment of God is made of none effect” (LJL 17.1).

Verse 11 thus stands as the actual heavenly courtroom in which the books-and-book-of-life adjudication of vv.12-15 takes place.

modernReadings.html: **Same — the post-millennial executive-judgment scene parallel to Rev 6:14-17, with the same "_earth and heaven flee away_" diction.** Verbatim: "**The third cycle begins with the second coming of Jesus right before the millennium begins. Jesus comes sitting on a white throne: 'Then I saw a great white throne and Him who sat on it, from whose face the heaven and the earth fled away and there was found no place for them.' Revelation 20:11 is parallel to Revelation 6:14-17. In both Jesus is sitting on a throne and in both the heavens recede as a scroll and the earth flees away**" (p. 272). NOTE: Bohr's framing at p. 272 reads the third cycle as beginning with "_the second coming of Jesus right before the millennium begins_" — but the verse he quotes is the great-white-throne post-millennial scene; the cycle-recapitulation framework allows him to overlap the pre-millennial and post-millennial earth-shaking events as the same kind-of-event (the Rev 6:14-17 Second-Coming earthquake / mountain-island removal is the type, and the Rev 20:11 post-millennial earth-and-heaven-fleeing is the antitype). On the post-millennial scope: "**This text describes the millennial judgment of the wicked. At this point the righteous will be in heaven and the wicked will all be dead on earth**" (p. 273). Convergent with Smith on the great-white-throne identification, the parallel to Rev 6:14-17, and the executive-judgment locale.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Rule V (Rev 6:14-17 / Rev 20:11 parallel diction). Rule IV (the 2 Pet 3:7-13 melting-of-elements + Rev 20:11 fleeing-away + Rev 21:1 first-heaven-and-earth-passed-away chain). Bohr's framing remark that the cycle "_begins with the second coming of Jesus right before the millennium begins_" is loose but not symbol-redefining — the great-white-throne event is unambiguously placed at the post-millennial executive judgment in his next paragraph. Convergent.

verses.text: And I saw the dead, small and great, stand before God; and the books were opened; and another book was opened, which is the book of life: and the dead were judged out of those things which were written in the books, according to their works.

pioneerReadings.html:
EGW quotes Rev 20:11-12 in full at GC 666.1 as the post-millennial executive-judgment scene, applies the same verse to the pre-Advent investigative judgment at GC 480-481, and explains the two-application principle as “the process will be similar”.

The pre-Advent application: “I saw a great white throne, and him that sat on it, from whose face the earth and the heaven fled away… And I saw the dead, small and great, stand before God; and the books were opened… the books were opened, including the book of life… the names of all who have ever served Him are recorded” (cf. GC 480-481).

The post-millennial application: “after they receive their harps, their robes, and their crowns, and enter the city, Jesus and the saints sit in judgment. The books are opened—the book of life and the book of death. The book of life contains the good deeds of the saints; and the book of death contains the evil deeds of the wicked. These books are compared with the statute book, the Bible, and according to that men are judged. The saints, in unison with Jesus” (EW 52.2). The same record-mechanism applies; the millennial-judgment-of-the-wicked is the saints’ judicial work in heaven from the throne-side, of which the great-white-throne final pronouncement (GC 666.1) is the executive culmination.

**The wicked dead stand to receive sentence, the books containing the record of all deeds.** Verbatim on the books: "**They are judged out of the things written in the books, from which we learn the solemn fact that a record of all our deeds is kept on high. A faithful and unerring record is made by the angelic secretaries. The wicked cannot conceal from them any of their deeds of darkness. They cannot bribe them to pass over in their record any of their unlawful acts. They must meet them all again, and be judged accordingly**" (DAR 699.2). On the punishment-according-to-works principle: "**They are to be punished according to their works. The Scriptures declare that they shall be rewarded according to their deeds. There are, then, to be degrees in the punishment of the wicked**" (DAR 699.3). On the book of life: "**Why, it may be asked, is the book of life brought forth on this occasion, when all who have part in the second resurrection, beyond which this scene is located, are already forejudged to the second death? One apparent reason, at least, is, That it may be seen that none of the names of all the multitude who die the second death are in the book of life, and why they are not there; and if the names have ever been there, why they were not retained; that all the intelligences of the universe may see that God acts with strict justice and impartiality**" (DAR 701.1).

Miller offers a surprising chronological reading of verse 12: he places the books-and-book-of-life scene at the beginning of the judgment. “It is very evident that this is the beginning of the judgment, when Christ comes in the clouds of heaven, with power and great glory, to raise and judge his saints, and to reward every man as his work shall be. 1st, because it is when the judgment first sets; 2nd, because the book of life is there, and open; and, 3rd because it was at the time or before antichrist was destroyed; and no one can believe that the anti christian beast can be on the earth during or in the millennium” (MWV2 33.1).

This is a strong Miller distinctive. He chains v.12 to Dan 7:10 (“the judgment was set, and the books were opened”) and to 2 Cor 5:10 / Rom 14:10 to argue that the saints’ works are reviewed at the first resurrection, even though justification flows from the book of life.

Divergence flag: Miller’s “judgment begins with the saints” reading is structurally identical to the later Sabbatarian pre-Advent judgment doctrine (developed by Crosier and Edson after 1844), though Miller locates it at the second advent itself rather than at 1844. The Sabbatarian refinement separates the two phases temporally; Miller collapses them. Yet the conceptual seed — investigative review of every saint’s record at judgment-set — is unmistakably in MWV2 33.1.

Litch reads verse 12 as the open courtroom of the second resurrection. Having argued that the Bible recognizes “two orders only, ‘the just and unjust;’ ‘the resurrection of life,’ and ‘the resurrection of damnation’” (PREX2 118.1), he uses v.12 to anchor the second order’s adjudication.

The procedure is forensic: “The resurrection of life is the first,—for on those who have part in it the second death hath no power; while those who have not their names in the book of life, will be cast into the lake of fire,—it will be the resurrection of damnation” (PREX2 118.1). The books of works of v.12 supply the evidentiary record; the book of life supplies the criterion.

Litch’s polemical use of v.12 is against any reading that collapses the two resurrections into one event. “No theory can harmonize the Bible on this point, except that of two resurrections; that does harmonize every text, and must be the true one” (PREX2 118.1). Verse 12 is therefore the courtroom of the second resurrection specifically — the bench where the wicked dead, raised at the close of the thousand years, are sentenced before the lake-of-fire executive act of v.15.

Andrews reads verse 12 as the post-millennial bench at which the third-angel’s second-phase penalty is publicly adjudicated. “The seven last plagues are poured out on the living wicked; but the second part of the penalty… is not inflicted until the end of the thousand years, when all the wicked are raised and suffer together” (TMR 124.2).

The opened books of works supply the legal record for every wicked person now standing in the second resurrection. The book of life remains the criterion: those whose names are absent receive the lake-of-fire sentence of v.15, while the books of works adjudicate the degree and duration of suffering. Andrews’s framing — “each is to be punished according to his deserts… that punishment will finally result in the utter destruction of all transgressors” (TMR 126.2) — presupposes precisely this two-book judicial process.

The structural point is procedural: verse 12 is not a generic homiletic image but the actual courtroom procedure at the close of the millennium. It is the legal venue where the third-angel warning of Rev 14:9-11 receives its executive enactment, and where the conditional-immortality verdict is rendered: judgment proportional to works, with annihilation the universal terminus.

Haskell’s most distinctive contribution to v.12 is his three-books framework: the Book of Remembrance (Mal 3:16; Matt 12:36-37) records every word; the Book of Life (Luke 10:20) holds the names of all who profess Christ; the Book of Death (Hos 13:12; Job 14:17) lists the sins of those who chose death.

“During the life of each individual, angels are recording the thoughts and the acts. These things are placed in a book, called by Malachi, the ‘Book of Remembrance.’ This is the daybook of heaven, and in it are recorded not only the words and deeds, but the circumstances and motives which prompted the acts” (SSP 330.2).

“These three books—the Book of Life of the Lamb, the Book of Remembrance, and the Book of Death are often referred to by the inspired writer. When the investigative judgment began in 1844, the Book of Life was opened; and before the Father, Christ pleaded His own blood for every name for which pardon was written” (SSP 333.3). The v.12 “books” (plural) and “another book… the book of life” Haskell maps onto this three-book structure. Convergent with Smith on the punishment-according-to-works principle and on the book of life’s role in the universe’s vindication of God’s justice.

Fitch’s literalist hermeneutic insists the books of verse 12 are real judicial records. “I take the plainly revealed word of God, in this chapter, for truth, and know of no reason for doing otherwise… If the resurrections spoken of in the twentieth chapter of the Revelation, are not to take place, literally, as therein laid down, then it is utterly impossible to prove from the Bible that there ever will be a resurrection” (LJL 14.3).

The same rule applied to vv.4-5 must apply to v.12: the books are not metaphors for divine memory, and the book of life is not a homiletic figure for evangelical sentiment. “I believe these things because ‘thus saith the Lord,’ in the twentieth chapter of Revelation, and because I know of no shadow of a reason for calling them mysticisms” (LJL 17.1).

This produces a fully forensic reading: the dead, small and great, literally stand before God; the books are literally opened; the book of life is literally a register; the judgment proceeds “according to their works” as a real adjudication. Fitch’s 1841 letter is one of the earliest American statements of this strict-literal courtroom reading of Rev 20:12, which the Sabbatarians later integrated into the pre-Advent investigative judgment framework.

modernReadings.html: **Same — millennial judgment of the wicked in absentia (the wicked are still dead in their graves during the 1000 years), with the universe's vindication-of-God's-justice as the purpose of bringing the book of life into view.** Verbatim: "**This text describes the millennial judgment of the wicked. At this point the righteous will be in heaven and the wicked will all be dead on earth… In the second cycle we saw that judgment was committed to those who came forth in the first resurrection. Revelation 20:12 describes how that judgment transpired during the millennium**" (p. 273). On the in-absentia mechanism: "**It is obvious that dead people cannot literally stand before God in the judgment. The text explicitly states that the wicked dead stood before God through the record of their lives in the books**" (pp. 273-274). On the EGW double application of Rev 20:12: "**A few years ago when I taught that Revelation 20:12 refers to the millennial judgment of the wicked someone objected saying that Ellen White applies this verse to the pre-Advent investigative judgment rather than the millennial judgment. I pointed out that Ellen White does indeed apply this verse to the pre-Advent judgment (GC, p. 480) but she also applies it to the post-millennial judgment because the process will be similar (GC, pp. 665, 666)**" (p. 274). On the book of life: "**The book of life contains the names of all the saved (Revelation 3:5; 13:8; Philippians 4:3; Exodus 32:32; Daniel 12:1) and the reason why the book of life is brought to view in the post-millennial judgment is to show the wicked that their names are not in it (see Revelation 20:15)**" (p. 274). Convergent with Smith on every point.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Rule V (Rev 3:5 + Rev 13:8 + Phil 4:3 + Exod 32:32 + Dan 12:1 + Rev 20:12 + Rev 20:15 "_book of life_" chain). Rule IV (the millennial-judgment process distinguished from the pre-Advent investigative-judgment process, but the same "_books_" + "_book of life_" mechanism applies to both — EGW GC 480 + GC 665-666 chain). Convergent.

verses.text: And the sea gave up the dead which were in it; and death and hell delivered up the dead which were in them: and they were judged every man according to their works.

pioneerReadings.html:
EGW reads the second resurrection of v.13 as the universal-scope return of all the wicked dead to face the great-white-throne sentence. “At the close of the thousand years, Christ again returns to the earth. He is accompanied by the host of the redeemed and attended by a retinue of angels. As He descends in terrific majesty He bids the wicked dead arise to receive their doom. They come forth, a mighty host, numberless as the sands of the sea” (GC 662.1).

The grave gives them up in their pre-death state: “Then, in terrible, fearful majesty, Jesus called forth the wicked dead; and they came up with the same feeble, sickly bodies that went into the grave… At the first resurrection all came forth in immortal bloom; but at the second the marks of the curse are visible on all” (EW 292.1). The “sea… death and hell delivered up the dead” clause covers every place of disposal—no body escapes the second resurrection’s reach. The “judged every man according to their works” clause sets up the second-death execution of vv. 14-15.

Smith folds v.13 into the broader executive-judgment / post-millennial-resurrection block; the "_sea gave up the dead_" is the second resurrection in its universal scope (no body lost; the sea, death, and hell all surrender their occupants). The substance is in the v. 11 paragraph above: "**It is the great white throne of judgment, before which they are assembled to receive their awful sentence of condemnation and death**" (DAR 697.3). On the judged-according-to-works principle: "**They are to be punished according to their works… There are, then, to be degrees in the punishment of the wicked**" (DAR 699.3).

Miller takes verse 13 as the executive gathering of the wicked dead from every domain that has held them. “I conclude the apostle, after he had seen the righteous dead raised… he then glides down to the end of the thousand years, and beheld the wicked dead given up by those elements and places wherein they had been confined during the millennial period, to be judged in the flesh, every man according to his works” (MWV2 33.1).

He parses the constituents: “By the sea, death and hell, I understand the sea, grave, and place of punishment. The sea and the grave would give up the dissolved particles of the body, and hell (or Hades) would give up their departed spirits; this would constitute the second resurrection” (MWV2 34.2).

Two payoffs follow. First, verse 13 is for Miller the procedural completion of the second resurrection itself — the moment the wicked dead are bodied and arraigned. Second, the “death and hell” phrase is taken as compound (body + spirit), not as personification, in line with his literalist rule. This sets up v.14 cleanly: when “death and hell” are cast into the lake of fire, Miller reads it as the executive destruction of body and spirit together — the “second death” in its full conditionalist force.

Litch reads verse 13 as the universalizing clause of the second resurrection. Because the Bible recognizes “two orders only, ‘the just and unjust’” (PREX2 118.1), the sea, death, and hell delivering up their dead must be the second order’s comprehensive rising — no wicked person is overlooked, none lost to the deep.

The judgment formula (“every man according to their works”) is for Litch the corollary of the book-of-life criterion: “Those who have not their names in the book of life, will be cast into the lake of fire,—it will be the resurrection of damnation” (PREX2 118.1). The works-record specifies the degree; the book-of-life absence specifies the verdict.

The structural payoff: verse 13 closes the gap that two-resurrection skeptics try to exploit. Critics had argued that if the “first resurrection” of v.5 is literal but applies only to martyrs, the rest of the wicked have no resurrection at all. Litch’s reading of v.13 forecloses that: the sea, death, and hell render up everyone outside the first resurrection, and they are all judicially processed before the executive sentence of vv.14-15.

Andrews uses verse 13 to assert the universal scope of the post-millennial judgment. “The seven last plagues are poured out on the living wicked; but the second part of the penalty… is not inflicted until the end of the thousand years, when all the wicked are raised and suffer together” (TMR 124.2). The sea, death, and hell giving up their dead is the comprehensive sweep that brings “all the wicked” into the executive courtroom.

Adjudication is “every man according to their works,” which Andrews weights against the popular eternal-torment reading: “Each is to be punished according to his deserts… All the wicked will God destroy. Psalm 145:20. They shall die the second death… they shall perish, being consumed into smoke… being burned up in unquenchable fire” (TMR 126.2, citing Ps 145:20; Rev 21:8; Ps 37:10, 20, 38; Mal 4:1).

The point is precise: verse 13 universalizes the second-resurrection assembly, but the works-judgment that follows produces graduated suffering and uniform terminal annihilation, not eternal conscious torment. The verse is therefore both an inclusion clause (none escape) and an apportionment clause (each according to deeds), with v.14-15 supplying the executive verdict.

Haskell reads v.13’s “sea gave up the dead… death and hell delivered up the dead” as the universal-scope second resurrection. “At the voice of God, the earth gave up the dead, who have long slept in her bosom. ‘The sea gave up the dead which were in it; and death and the grave delivered up the dead which were in them.’ They rise to see the holy city” (SSP 334.2).

The second resurrection occurs on the post-millennial earth (since the descended city is already there, and the wicked rise to behold it). No place—sea, grave, hell (sheol/hades)—retains its dead through the executive-judgment scene. The “judged every man according to their works” clause Haskell links to the three-books judicial record he establishes at SSP 330.2-333.3: the millennial-judgment-in-absentia verdict is now executed at the moment of resurrection.

Fitch reads verse 13 as the second-resurrection delivery scene, in line with his literalist rule. Because “the two resurrections, spoken of in the twentieth chapter of the Revelation, will take place literally, as there laid down” (LJL 14.2), the sea, death, and hell rendering up their dead must be the actual bodily resurrection of every wicked person at the close of the thousand years.

His chain links v.13 directly to vv.7-9 as the same composite event: “I believe this Gog and Magog to be ‘the rest of the dead,’ who ‘live not again until the thousand years are finished’—that is, all the wicked; that these will then go up on all the breadth of the earth; i. e., will then be raised from the dead and compass the camp of the saints about, and the beloved city” (LJL 16.2).

The post-millennial resurrection is therefore a single comprehensive event seen from two angles: v.7-9 shows the result (the gathered army before the city); v.13 shows the procedure (the elements yielding their dead). Fitch’s framing keeps the literal grammar intact and refuses to let “death and hell” be allegorized into a generic statement about mortality.

modernReadings.html: **The post-millennial resurrection of the wicked, occurring on earth, with the books-of-record verdict applied at the moment of resurrection so each lost person sees why they are excluded from the city.** Verbatim: "**Revelation 20:13 describes the post-millennial resurrection of the wicked to witness the reason why God excluded them from the city: 'The sea gave up the dead who were in it, and Death and Hades delivered up the dead who were in them. And they were judged, each one according to his works.' The expressions 'gave up' and 'delivered up' refer to the resurrection of the dead who were judged in absentia during the thousand years**" (p. 274). The Isa 26:19 cross-link: "**Isaiah 26:19 uses a similar expression to refer to the resurrection: 'Your dead shall live; together with my dead body they shall arise. Awake and sing, you who dwell in dust; for your dew is like the dew of herbs, and the earth shall cast out the dead'**" (p. 274). On the locale: "**We know that the judgment in Revelation 20:13 occurs on earth because, according to verses 7-9 the wicked will surround the New Jerusalem on earth after the millennium**" (p. 274). And on the saved-by-grace-judged-by-works principle: "**Although we are saved by grace through faith (Ephesians 2:8, 9), we are judged by works because our works reveal whether our faith is genuine. In the judgment, the entire universe will see that not only were the incorrigibly wicked condemned by their works, but also many who claimed to follow Jesus and said, 'Lord, Lord' but whose lives contradicted their profession**" (pp. 274-275). Convergent with Smith on the universal scope of the second resurrection and the judged-by-works mechanism.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Rule V (Isa 26:19 + Rev 20:13 resurrection-from-dust-and-sea cross-link). Rule IV (the second-resurrection-on-earth locale is fixed by the Rev 20:7-9 city-on-earth chronology). Convergent.

verses.text: And death and hell were cast into the lake of fire. This is the second death.

pioneerReadings.html:
EGW reads v.14’s “this is the second death” as the textual self-gloss that secures the annihilationist reading of the entire chapter’s closing scene. “Said the angel, ‘Satan is the root, his children are the branches. They are now consumed root and branch. They have died an everlasting death. They are never to have a resurrection, and God will have a clean universe’” (EW 295.1).

The duration: “I saw that some were quickly destroyed, while others suffered longer. They were punished according to the deeds done in the body. Some were many days consuming, and just as long as there was a portion of them unconsumed, all the sense of suffering remained. Said the angel, ‘The worm of life shall not die; their fire shall not be quenched as long as there is the least particle for it to prey upon’” (EW 294.1).

Even death and hell themselves are destroyed: “In the cleansing flames the wicked are at last destroyed, root and branch—Satan the root, his followers the branches. The full penalty of the law has been visited; the demands of justice have been met; and heaven and earth, beholding, declare the righteousness of Jehovah” (GC 673.1). The second death is total, final, unrepeatable; death itself is abolished (cf. 1 Cor 15:26; Rev 21:4).

**The final epitaph of all the forces of evil — death itself, hell itself, all opposition, annihilated.** Verbatim: "**'And death and hell were cast into the lake of fire. This is the second death.' This is the final epitaph of all the forces that have risen up from first to last, to oppose the will and work of the Lord Almighty. Satan originated and led out in this nefarious work. A portion of heaven's angels joined him in his false position and murderous work; and for him and them the everlasting fire was prepared. Matthew 25:41. Men become involved therein only because they join him in his rebellion. But here the controversy closes. The fire is to them everlasting because it allows of no escape. The second death is their punishment, and it is. 'everlasting punishment' (Matthew 25:46) because they never find release from its dread embrace. 'The wages of sin is death'**" (DAR 701.2). The "_everlasting_" is the everlasting result, not the duration of conscious suffering — "**it allows of no escape**" + "**they never find release**".

Miller’s gloss on verse 14 is brief but exegetically loaded: “By death and hell I understand the body and spirit.” (MWV2 34.2). This compound reading treats “death” as standing for the bodily remains rendered up in v.13 and “hell” (Hades) as standing for the departed spirit — both reunited in the second resurrection only to be cast into the lake of fire.

The consequence is structurally conditionalist, though Miller does not press the polemic. If “death and hell” means body and spirit, the casting into the lake of fire is the executive destruction of the whole person, not merely the cessation of bodily life. He explicitly identifies this with “the second death,” pairing it with Rev 21:8 (“the fearful and unbelieving… shall have their part in the lake which burneth with fire and brimstone, which is the second death”) in the same paragraph (MWV2 34.2).

The Sabbatarian refinement will sharpen the conditionalist payoff (Andrews TMR 126.2, Haskell SSP 336.1), but the seed is here: the second death terminates the dualistic body/spirit composite, not merely the body. Miller’s exegesis on v.14 is therefore one of the early American formulations of the “destruction of the whole person” doctrine, even while his lecture on v.10 still uses inherited “everlasting torment” language.

Litch reads verse 14 as the executive enactment of the “resurrection of damnation” verdict. Having argued that the two orders of resurrection — life and damnation — exhaust the biblical categories, he treats the lake-of-fire casting as the procedural completion of the second order: “Those who have not their names in the book of life, will be cast into the lake of fire,—it will be the resurrection of damnation” (PREX2 118.1).

The compound “death and hell” mirrors v.13: what was given up there to enable judgment is now destroyed. The second death is therefore not a meaningless reduplication of the first death but its irrevocable counterpart — the death from which there is no resurrection.

Litch’s framing keeps the verse procedurally tight: v.13 assembles, v.14 destroys, v.15 names the criterion. The whole arc serves his polemic against any reading that collapses the two resurrections or denies the literal force of the chapter’s judicial language. Verse 14 is therefore the moment death itself is unmade — the last enemy abolished — in the executive fire that closes the great-white-throne adjudication.

James White (citing Miller’s own narrative in SLWM) ties verse 14 directly to the destruction sequence at the second advent and its post-millennial aftermath. “The bodies of the wicked will then be all destroyed, and their spirits be reserved in prison and damnation” until the post-millennial sentence (SLWM 52.1, citing Rev 20:3, 13-15).

Verse 14 is the executive moment that resolves that “reservation”: the spirits, having been held in custody during the thousand years, are now reunited with raised bodies (v.13), judged (v.12), and destroyed (v.14). The compound “death and hell” cast into the lake of fire is for White the abolition of both the bodily and spiritual modes of post-mortem confinement.

This is structurally important for the Sabbatarian polemic against universalism and against eternal-torment dualism alike. Verse 14 closes both options: there is no universal salvation (the wicked are explicitly destroyed) and no eternal torture chamber (“death and hell” themselves are unmade in the lake of fire). The second death is therefore the final dying — the executive cancellation of death itself, fulfilling 1 Cor 15:26 and clearing the way for the new heavens and new earth in which death is no more (Rev 21:4).

Andrews reads v.14’s “this is the second death” as the integrating self-definition of the lake-of-fire / final-conflagration scene. “‘But the fearful and unbelieving, and the abominable, and murderers, and whoremongers, and sorcerers, and idolaters, and all liars, shall have their part in the lake which burneth with fire and brimstone; which is the second death.’ Revelation 21:8” (cited TMR 124.4 as a definitional parallel).

The annihilation conclusion: “however dreadful and long-continued the punishment of the wicked will be (for each is to be punished according to his deserts), that punishment will finally result in the utter destruction of all transgressors. All the wicked will God destroy. They shall die the second death… They shall perish, being consumed into smoke… They shall be punished with everlasting destruction, being burned up in unquenchable fire… And thus, having been consumed, root and branch, they shall be as though they had not been” (TMR 126.2). The casting of death and hell into the lake of fire (v.14a) Andrews reads as the elimination of the conditions under which the wicked existed—the grave itself loses its tenants permanently. Convergent with Smith on second-death = annihilation.

Haskell reads verse 14 as the typological terminus of the Day-of-Atonement ritual. “Then from His throne, God breathes upon the assembled multitudes. Fire comes down from God out of heaven, and mingles with the fire which comes from the interior of the earth; and it devours them. The devil that deceived them… was cast into the lake of fire and brimstone, where the beast and the false prophet are. And death and hell were cast into the lake of fire… This is the second death” (SSP 336.1).

The Levitical reading is explicit: “The wicked become ashes under the soles of the feet of the righteous. The last act in the shadowy service of the tabernacle,—the placing of the ashes of the bullock in a clean place,—has met its antitype” (SSP 336.1, alluding to Lev 16:27-28).

Two payoffs follow. First, the lake of fire is the earth’s own surface during the final conflagration — not a separately existing torture chamber. Second, “death and hell” being cast in is the terminal abolition of death itself, fulfilling 1 Cor 15:26 (“the last enemy that shall be destroyed is death”). Haskell’s framework is fully conditionalist: the second death is the second and final dying, after which existence is forever blotted out.

Fitch reads verse 14 as the literal terminal sentence on the second resurrection. “Fire will come down from God out of heaven and devour them; that the devil that deceived them will be cast into the lake of fire, where the beast and the false prophet are, and will be tormented day and night forever and ever; and that this is the second death” (LJL 16.2).

The literal grammar holds at every joint: the lake is real fire, the casting is real consignment, the second death is real termination. Fitch’s refusal to mysticize means he cannot construe “death and hell were cast into the lake of fire” as anything less than the executive abolition of death and the grave themselves.

Methodologically: Fitch’s 1841 letter is one of the earliest American statements that locks the “second death” identification of v.14 to a fully literal reading of vv.4-9. Where many of his contemporaries spiritualized the second death into “separation from God,” Fitch insists on the plain referent — the lake of fire of v.14 is the second death, and the second death is the actual destruction prepared for those outside the book of life of v.15. The chapter’s internal coherence (his standing argument against mystical interpreters) requires this reading.

modernReadings.html: **Same — second death = annihilation; the climax of vv. 14-15 reaches the same point as vv. 8-9 (one lake of fire, one final event).** Verbatim: "**After the wicked have had their day in court, God will cast them into the lake of fire and they will suffer second death: 'Then Death and Hades were cast into the lake of fire. This is the second death. And anyone not found written in the Book of Life was cast into the lake of fire.' Revelation 20:14, 15 reaches the same climax as we saw in Revelation 20:8, 9. After all, there are not two lakes of fire! After Satan and his angels, the wicked, and death and the grave are consumed in the lake of fire, God will create a new heaven and a new earth because the first heaven and earth have passed away**" (pp. 275-276). And on second-death-as-annihilation cross-reference at the volume's wider treatment: "**suffering of the wicked in the fire as second death, Revelation 20:14, 15 does:**" (p. 10531). Fully convergent with Smith.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Rule IV (Mal 4:1, 3 + Ezek 28:18-19 + Matt 25:41 + Rev 2:11 + Rev 20:14 + Rev 21:8 chain — the second-death = annihilation reading is the only one consistent across all the texts). Rule V (the one-lake-of-fire identity between vv. 8-9 and vv. 14-15 is forced by the text itself — "_there are not two lakes of fire_"). Convergent.

verses.text: And whosoever was not found written in the book of life was cast into the lake of fire.

pioneerReadings.html:
Ellen White reads verse 15 as the closing executive sentence of the great-white-throne judgment, not as proof of conscious eternal burning. She quotes the verse alongside v.11-12 to frame Christ’s “final coronation” before “the assembled inhabitants of earth and heaven” (GC 666.1), where “the King of kings pronounces sentence upon the rebels.”

For her the lake of fire is not an ever-burning torture chamber but the earth’s own surface during the final conflagration: “The earth’s surface seems one molten mass—a vast, seething lake of fire… In the cleansing flames the wicked are at last destroyed, root and branch—Satan the root, his followers the branches” (GC 672.2, 673.1). The absence of a name in the book of life is therefore the legal warrant for annihilation, not for unending torment; “some are destroyed as in a moment, while others suffer many days. All are punished according to their deeds” (GC 673.1).

Verse 15 thus seals the conditional-immortality position: life is granted only to those written in the Lamb’s book, and every other will and memory is extinguished in the fire that purifies the planet for re-creation.

Smith closes the chapter with a pastoral application — the book of life is the deciding register, and the reader is exhorted to make sure their name is there. Verbatim: "**'And whosoever was not found written in the book of life was cast into the lake of fire.' Reader, is your name written in the book of life? Are you striving to avert in your own case the fearful doom that awaits the ungodly? Rest not till you have reason to believe that your name is registered in the list of those who are to share at last in the blessings of eternal life**" (DAR 701.3). Smith does not give v.15 a separate doctrinal exegesis beyond the book-of-life register; the substance is in the v.12 + v.14 treatments above.

Miller reads verse 15 alongside Rev 21:8 as the definitive verdict on the second resurrection. Having argued that the books of v.12 record the works of the wicked while the book of life adjudicates the righteous, he concludes: “Whosoever was not found written in the Lamb’s book of life, was cast into the lake of fire… which burneth with fire and brimstone, which is the second death” (MWV2 34.2).

For Miller this verse closes Lecture 2 by sealing the contrast between the two resurrections: those in the first “shall be priests of God and of Christ, and shall reign with him a thousand years” (MWV2 35.1), while those absent from the book of life suffer the irrevocable second death. He treats the book-of-life criterion as the only escape from the lake of fire and links it directly to commandment-keeping via Rev 22:14: “Blessed are they that do his commandments, that they may have right to the tree of life, and may enter in through the gates into the city” (MWV2 34.2).

Divergence flag: Miller’s 1842 framework still uses the inherited “tormented forever” language of v.10 in passing, but his exegesis here drives toward terminal sentence rather than conscious eternal pain — the Sabbatarians later sharpened this into a fully conditionalist reading.

Litch reads verse 15 as the closing line of the “two resurrections” argument. Because “the blessed and holy have part in the first resurrection” and on them the second death has no power, the negative corollary is that “those who have not their names in the book of life, will be cast into the lake of fire,—it will be the resurrection of damnation” (PREX2 118.1).

He insists that the Bible recognizes two and only two orders — “the just and unjust,” “the resurrection of life,” and “the resurrection of damnation” (PREX2 118.1). Verse 15 therefore is not a third class but the executive sentence on the second order: the absence of one’s name in the Lamb’s book is the legal warrant for the second death.

Litch ties this back to v.6’s deliverance language: the saints “shall be delivered from the temptations of the devil, who shall be chained… and even then can have no power on those who have part in the first resurrection” (PREX2 117.2). Verse 15 is the symmetrical opposite: those outside the book of life are not delivered — they are devoured.

James White (citing Miller’s own narrative in SLWM) treats verse 15 as the sealing clause of the only millennium the Bible teaches. The bodies of the wicked “will then be all destroyed, and their spirits be reserved in prison and damnation” until the post-millennial sentence (SLWM 52.1, citing Rev 20:3, 13-15 in the chain).

White preserves Miller’s framing: “The only millennium taught in the word of God is the thousand years which are to intervene between the first resurrection and that of the rest of the dead… and that it must necessarily follow the personal coming of Christ and the regeneration of the earth” (SLWM 53.1). Verse 15 therefore stands at the far end of that thousand-year interval: the second resurrection’s wicked, lacking any entry in the book of life, receive the same fate as the beast and false prophet of v.10.

This is structurally important: White uses verse 15 to refute the popular post-millennialist “temporal millennium”, since a converted-world reading cannot account for a final lake-of-fire executive judgment that presupposes unconverted millions still requiring sentence at the thousand years’ close.

Andrews reads verse 15 as the climax of the third angel’s warning — the moment the second part of the penalty falls. He distinguishes the seven last plagues (poured out on the living wicked) from the lake-of-fire sentence, which “is not inflicted until the end of the thousand years, when all the wicked are raised and suffer together” (TMR 124.2).

The criterion is the book of life: “The fearful and unbelieving, and the abominable, and murderers… shall have their part in the lake which burneth with fire and brimstone; which is the second death” (Rev 21:8, quoted at TMR 124.4). Andrews then nails the conditionalist conclusion: “However dreadful and long-continued the punishment of the wicked will be… that punishment will finally result in the utter destruction of all transgressors. All the wicked will God destroy… they shall die the second death… they shall perish, being consumed into smoke… having been consumed, root and branch, they shall be as though they had not been” (TMR 126.2, citing Ps 145:20; Rev 21:8; Rom 6:23; Ps 37:10, 20, 38; Mal 4:1).

Verse 15 is therefore the executive sentence on the third-angel warning, not a proof-text for conscious eternal torment.

Haskell reads verse 15 as the antitypical placing of the ashes of the bullock outside the camp. God breathes fire from the throne, it mingles with the fire from the earth’s interior, “and it devours them. The devil that deceived them was cast into the lake of fire and brimstone… And death and hell were cast into the lake of fire… And whosoever was not found written in the Book of Life was cast into the lake of fire. This is the second death” (SSP 336.1).

For Haskell the New Jerusalem “rides safely on the billows of flame” like the ark in the flood. “The wicked become ashes under the soles of the feet of the righteous. The last act in the shadowy service of the tabernacle,—the placing of the ashes of the bullock in a clean place,—has met its antitype” (SSP 336.1, alluding to Lev 16:27-28). The book of life is the sole admission to the city; everyone else is consumed in the cleansing fire that prepares the new earth.

The doctrinal payoff: the second death is real, terminal, and re-creational. “The enemy of truth, together with all who have championed his cause, is forever blotted out of existence: the earth is ready to be renewed” (SSP 336.1).

Fitch’s 1841 Letter to Litch arrives at verse 15 through a strictly literal grammar. Having argued that “the two resurrections, spoken of in the twentieth chapter of the Revelation, will take place literally, as there laid down” (LJL 14.2), he reads Gog and Magog as “the rest of the dead” raised at the close of the thousand years: “fire will come down from God out of heaven and devour them; that the devil that deceived them will be cast into the lake of fire… and that this is the second death” (LJL 16.2).

Verse 15 functions as the explanatory rider on this scene. Fitch refuses any mystical or figurative escape: “I believe these things because ‘thus saith the Lord,’ in the twentieth chapter of Revelation, and because I know of no shadow of a reason for calling them mysticisms. The opinion that they are mysticisms, I believe to be one of those groundless traditions of men, by which the commandment of God is made of none effect” (LJL 17.1).

The book-of-life clause is therefore not symbolic admittance but the actual legal register that decides who is devoured in the post-millennial fire. This is the same conditionalist edge later sharpened by Andrews and Haskell.

modernReadings.html: **Same — the book of life is the register; absence of a name = casting into the lake of fire = second-death annihilation.** Verbatim: "**The names of the lost will not be in the Book of Life because of the record of their lives that were written in the books. In the third cycle we do not yet know what those works were. The fourth cycle will reveal some of the sins that excluded the wicked from the Book of Life. It is remarkable that God will not destroy the wicked until they have had their day in court and agree that God was right in excluding them from the Book of Life**" (p. 274). And the quote of vv. 14-15 at p. 275 (cited above under v.14). Bohr extends the application forward into the four-cycle's fourth-cycle treatment of who is inside the city (the overcomers of Rev 21:7) vs who is outside (the second-death class of Rev 21:8) — a Rule-V intra-vision chain. Convergent.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Rule V (Rev 20:12 + Rev 20:15 + Rev 21:8 + Rev 21:27 + Rev 22:15 "_outside the city_" chain — the same class is described at the chapter's close as at the closing verses of the whole book; the book-of-life register is the deciding factor). Convergent.

summaryHtml:
**Rev 20 violation summary (against _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 261-279, with cross-coverage at pp. 537, 541, 3367-3370, 5911, 6017, 6029-6138, 6568, 8756-8838, 10133-10144, 10531, 10939, 11007-11260):** Of 15 verses, Bohr has **zero rule violations**. Rev 20 is, with Rev 11 and Rev 15, one of the **most rule-compliant chapters in Bohr's entire Revelation 12-22 corpus**. Every primary identification is fully convergent with Smith — abyss = pre-creation chaos of the desolate earth (Gen 1:2 LXX _abussos_ = Rev 20:1 _abussos_); angel of v.1 ≠ Christ (the scapegoat-typology executor); binding of Satan = no subjects left to deceive (the chain-of-circumstances reading, paralleled to Rev 13:9-10 captivity by loss of power-base); first resurrection = righteous-only, at the Second Coming, the saints translated to the Father's house in heaven (John 14:1-3); saints reign and judge in heaven 1000 years (1 Cor 6:1-3 invoked explicitly); Rev 20:5b parenthetical = the basis for the two-resurrection doctrine; second death = annihilation, not eternal conscious torment; Satan loosed = the second resurrection giving him his power-base back; Gog and Magog = the resurrected wicked of all ages (NOT a modern geopolitical actor — a key Rule-XI / Rule-V restraint shared with Smith and against dispensational schemes); New Jerusalem descends to earth before the wicked surround it (Rev 21:2 chronologically before Rev 21:1); fire from God = the executive-judgment annihilation; the lake of fire of vv. 14-15 = the same event as the fire-from-God of vv. 8-9 ("_there are not two lakes of fire_"); the beast and false prophet of v.10 = the organizations cast in at the Second Coming (Rev 19:20), the individuals of which come up in the second resurrection as Gog/Magog. Bohr's distinctive structural extensions are **Rule-VI recapitulation amplifications, not divergences**: (a) the **four-cycle recapitulation framework** for Rev 20:1-21:8 (Cycle 1 = 20:1-3 Satan + earth; Cycle 2 = 20:4-10 the saints; Cycle 3 = 20:11-21:1 judgment of the wicked; Cycle 4 = 21:2-8 the New Jerusalem) — pure Rule-VI repetition-by-different-figures; (b) the **Rev 13:9-10 papal-captivity / Rev 20:2 Satan-binding parallel** as the mechanism for "captivity by loss of power-base" — a Rule-V chain explaining the figurative "binding"; (c) the **Gen 1:2 LXX / Jer 4:23 / Isa 24 de-creation chain** for the abyss-as-desolate-earth identification — a Rule-V chain making explicit what Smith assumes; (d) the **Rev 21:2-before-Rev-21:1 chronological inversion** (the city descends before the fire-from-God destroys the earth) — a Rule-IV chronology-fix from cross-reading the chapters. On every distinctive doctrinal point — saints in heaven during the 1000 years, two resurrections separated by 1000 years, annihilation of the wicked, no millennial saints-on-earth view, no dispensational Gog/Magog identification, no eternal conscious torment — pioneer SDA and Bohr are in **lockstep**. **Verdict: full convergence. Rev 20 is core SDA eschatology, and Bohr exposits it without drift.**
